safety_issues: Evaluation of terrain avoidance procedures for air carriers operating transport-category aircraft
Presentation of angle-of-attack information in a visual format to pilots and training on its use
recommendations: Evaluate the terrain avoidance procedures of air carriers operating transport-category aircraft to ensure that they provide maximum escape performance and are placed in procedural sections of approved operations manuals (A-96-93)

Require all transport-category aircraft to present pilots with angle-of-attack information in a visual format, and train pilots on its use for obtaining maximum possible airplane climb performance (A-96-94)
key_findings: The FAA agreed with the intent of evaluating terrain avoidance procedures and had developed CFIT Education and Training Aid along with Boeing
The FAA began an evaluation to assess operational requirements for angle-of-attack indicators, including implementation, training requirements, complexity, cost, and other functions

location: FE Newark International Airport Newark, New Jersey
probable_cause: The captain's overcontrol of the airplane during the landing and failure to execute a go-around from a destabilized flare.
contributing_factors: Captain's concern with touching down early to ensure adequate stopping distance.
safety_issues: Air carrier pilots' performance would be improved by additional guidance and training in landing techniques.

Some flight crewmembers may lack proficiency in the operation of airport performance laptop computers, or similar airplane performance computing devices, leading to potentially hazardous miscalculations of available runway distances after touchdown.
recommendations: Additional basic research is needed to identify undesirable landing phase combinations and compare the overall qualitative and quantitative stability and control characteristics of widely used large transport-category airplanes to improve certification criteria and reduce the incidence of potentially catastrophic landing accidents.

Air carriers transporting hazardous materials continue to need a means to quickly retrieve and provide consolidated, specific information to emergency responders about the identity of all hazardous materials on an airplane.
key_findings: The accident airplane performed normally in response to the captain's flight control inputs until after the second touchdown.

The energy transmitted into the right main landing gear during the second touchdown was sufficient to fully compress (bottom) the right main landing gear strut and cause structural failure of the right wing rear spar, leading to a fire.

location: Near East Moriches, New York July
probable_cause: The presence of explosive fuel/air vapor in the center wing tank (CWT) and an ignition source within or near the CWT that was not identified by the investigation.
contributing_factors: Operation with potentially explosive fuel/air mixtures in the CWT
Lack of adequate measures to prevent or control fuel tank explosions
safety_issues: Need for reducing or eliminating explosive fuel/air mixtures in aircraft fuel tanks
Necessity of addressing potential ignition sources within airplane fuel tanks
recommendations: Require that flight handbooks be revised to reflect accurate increases in CWT fuel temperatures during air conditioning pack operation on the ground
Implement operational changes to reduce exposure time to explosive fuel/air mixtures
Evaluate long-term solutions such as insulation or inerting systems for fuel tanks
key_findings: Flight tests showed significant rises in CWT temperature due to extended periods of air conditioning pack operation on the ground
Current FAA policy of preventing fuel tank explosions by eliminating ignition sources has not been 100% effective

location: Miles South of Colorado Springs Municipal Airport Colorado Springs, Colorado
probable_cause: A loss of control of the airplane resulting from the movement of the rudder surface to its blowdown limit. The rudder surface most likely deflected in a direction opposite to that commanded by the pilots as a result of a jam of the main rudder power control unit servo valve secondary slide to the servo valve housing offset from its neutral position and overtravel of the primary slide.
contributing_factors: Jamming of the main rudder power control unit servo valve secondary slide
Overtravel of the primary slide
safety_issues: Dual-concentric servo valve used in all Boeing 737 main rudder power control units is not reliably redundant.
Potential failure modes and malfunctions in the Boeing 737 rudder system that could lead to a loss of control.
recommendations: Rudder system design changes to preclude the rudder reversal failure mode
Development of training and piloting techniques to counteract uncommanded deflection of the rudder
key_findings: The flightcrew was certificated and qualified for the flight.
The airplane was properly certificated and maintained in accordance with existing regulations.
There were no air traffic control factors in the cause of the accident.

Analysis of cockpit voice recorder, NTSB computer simulation, and human performance data shows that a rudder reversal most likely caused by a jam of the main rudder power control unit servo valve secondary slide to the servo valve housing offset from its neutral position and overtravel of the primary slide.

probable_cause: The flight crew's failure to discontinue the approach when severe thunderstorms and their associated hazards to flight operations had moved into the airport area and the crew's failure to ensure that the spoilers had extended after touchdown.
contributing_factors: impaired performance resulting from fatigue
situational stress associated with the intent to land under the circumstances
continuation of the approach to a landing when the company's maximum crosswind component was exceeded
use of reverse thrust greater than 1.3 engine pressure ratio after landing
safety_issues: Weather-related safety recommendations
Fatigue-related safety recommendation
Lack of operational redundancy for spoiler arming before landing
Procedures to ensure that spoilers have deployed after touchdown should be a required part of all air carriers' landing operations
Need for improved weather information systems and availability
recommendations: Ensure flight crews discontinue approaches when severe thunderstorms are present in the airport area
Improve procedures to ensure spoiler deployment post-landing
Limit maximum reverse thrust for MD-80 landings on wet or slippery runways to 1.3 engine pressure ratio, except when directional control can be sacrificed for a marginal increase in deceleration
Enhance flight dispatchers' access to Terminal Doppler Weather Radar information and Weather Systems Processor information
Staff Center Weather Service Units at all times when significant weather is predicted to affect their areas of operation
Improve the reporting and archiving capability of Runway Visual Range data
key_findings: The flight crew was properly certificated and qualified under Federal and company requirements.
No evidence indicated preexisting medical or behavioral conditions that might have adversely affected the flight crew's performance during the accident flight.
Dynamic or reverted rubber hydroplaning did not occur during the landing rollout.
Autospoiler system operated properly, but spoilers did not automatically deploy because the spoiler handle was not armed by either pilot before landing.
The use of reverse thrust at levels greater than 1.3 engine pressure ratio significantly reduced the effectiveness of the airplane's rudder and vertical stabilizer.

location: Miles North of Anacapa Island, California
probable_cause: The worn threads inside the horizontal stabilizer acme nut were incrementally sheared off by the acme screw, leading to a jammed longitudinal trim control system and subsequent structural failures that resulted in loss of control.
contributing_factors: Insufficient lubrication of the jackscrew assembly
Alaska Airlines' extension of the lubrication interval for its McDonnell Douglas MD-80 horizontal stabilizer components
FAA's approval of Alaska Airlines' extended lubrication intervals
Extension and FAA approval of the end play check interval
safety_issues: Lack of clear guidance to flight crews on appropriate actions in case of an inoperative or malfunctioning flight control system
Inadequate maintenance procedures for jackscrew assembly lubrication and inspection
Potential influence from dispatch personnel on flight crew decision-making
recommendations: Clarify guidelines for pilots dealing with in-flight control problems to prevent unintended worsening of the condition
Improve maintenance procedures, including extending access panels and requiring inspector sign-off for jackscrew assembly lubrication tasks
Review and potentially revise FAA approval processes for extended maintenance intervals
key_findings: The flight crew was properly certified and qualified with no evidence of preexisting conditions affecting performance.
Weather, structural failures (other than those associated with the longitudinal trim control system), fire, or impact with foreign objects were not factors in the accident.
Both engines were operating normally before the final dive.
Air traffic control personnel involved were properly certified and qualified for their duty stations.
The horizontal stabilizer stopped responding to autopilot and pilot commands after passing through 23,400 feet due to a jammed longitudinal trim control system.
The initial and final dives were caused by the failure of the jackscrew assembly components and vertical stabilizer tip fairing brackets.

flight_type: Instrument Meteorological Conditions (IMC) flight under Part 91 regulations
probable_cause: The pilot's spatial disorientation resulting from his failure to maintain positive manual control of the airplane with the available flight instrumentation.
contributing_factors: Loss of a.c. electrical power during instrument meteorological conditions
safety_issues: Lack of significant oversight for team travel by Oklahoma State University
recommendations: Review Oklahoma State University's postaccident team travel policy and develop, either independently or jointly, a model policy for member institutions to use in creating a travel policy or strengthening an existing travel policy.
key_findings: The pilot was properly certificated and qualified under Federal regulations.
No evidence indicates any preexisting medical or behavioral conditions that might have adversely affected the pilot's performance during the accident flight.
The second pilot was not a required crewmember because the airplane was certified for single-pilot operation under 14 CFR Part 91.
The accident airplane was properly certified, equipped, and maintained in accordance with Federal regulations.
Icing was not a factor in this accident.
No evidence of a cabin pressurization problem was found.
A complete loss of a.c. electrical power occurred aboard the airplane.
The pilot did not appropriately manage the workload associated with troubleshooting the loss of a.c. electrical power with the need to establish and maintain positive control of the airplane.

probable_cause: A loss of pitch control resulting from the disconnection of the right elevator control tab, caused by the failure to properly secure and inspect the attachment bolt.
contributing_factors: Improper maintenance procedures during the most recent D inspection or subsequent maintenance.
Lack of emphasis on verifying symmetry between the right and left side elevators, control tabs, and geared tabs during preflight inspections.
DC-8 operators not using the elevator position indicator (EPI) to confirm elevator movement indications above and below the neutral range during the 80-knot elevator check.
safety_issues: The current DC-8 design does not preclude a catastrophic result from a disconnection or failure of the existing control tab crank fitting to pushrod attachment.
Use of outdated, incomplete, or otherwise unsuitable reference materials by maintenance personnel during installation and/or assembly of airplane components.
recommendations: Require all DC-8 operators to train flight crewmembers to look for symmetry between right and left side elevators, control tabs, and geared tabs during preflight inspection.
Develop and require incorporation of specific procedures for the 80-knot elevator check that ensure pilots are clearly made aware of whether the elevator is functioning properly before takeoff.
Require periodic calibration inspections of the EPI to ensure accurate indications observed by pilots.
Redesign DC-8 elevator control tab installations so that pilots can safely operate the airplane if the control tab becomes disconnected from the pushrod.
key_findings: The bolt connecting the right elevator control tab crank fitting to the pushrod migrated out of the fitting, allowing the control tab to disengage and shift to a trailing edge down position.

Due to the extreme trailing edge down deflection of the right elevator control tab, the airplane's elevators were driven into an extreme nose-up pitch attitude that could not be overcome by the pilots' efforts.

probable_cause: The flight crew's failure to maintain adequate airspeed, which led to an aerodynamic stall from which they did not recover.
contributing_factors: Flight crew failed to monitor the airplane’s airspeed and allowed it to decrease to a dangerously low level for about 50 seconds
Inadequate adherence to Aviation Charter's approach procedures and ineffective application of crew resource management techniques during the approach segment of the flight
safety_issues: CRM training in Part 135 operations
Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) surveillance adequacy for on-demand charter operators
Need for low-airspeed alert systems in commercial airplanes
recommendations: Conduct en route inspections and observe ground training, flight training, and proficiency checks at all Part 135 on-demand charter operations to ensure the adequacy, quality, and standardization of pilot training and flight operations.

Require that Part 135 on-demand charter operators that conduct dual-pilot operations establish and implement an FAA-approved crew resource management (CRM) training program for their flight crews in accordance with Part 121, subparts N and O.
Convene a panel to determine the feasibility of requiring low-airspeed alert systems in airplanes engaged in commercial operations under Parts 121 and 135.
Establish requirements for low-airspeed alert systems based on the findings of the panel if deemed feasible.
key_findings: The flight crew failed to maintain an appropriate course and speed for the approach, did not properly configure the airplane at the start of the approach, and allowed airspeed to decrease dangerously.
Both pilots had previously demonstrated potentially serious performance deficiencies during flight operations consistent with below-average flight proficiency.

Aviation Charter was not operating in accordance with its weight and balance load manifest procedures, lacked adequate stall recovery guidance, deicer boot operational guidance, and an in-range checklist.

probable_cause: The loss of control due to the incorrect rigging of the elevator control system during maintenance, which restricted downward elevator travel and combined with an aft center of gravity that rendered the airplane uncontrollable in pitch.
contributing_factors: Inadequate on-the-job training and supervision by Raytheon Aerospace quality assurance inspector
Failure to diligently follow the elevator control system rigging procedure as written
Lack of a complete functional check at the end of maintenance for critical flight systems or their components
Excessive aft center of gravity due to inaccurate weight and balance calculations
safety_issues: Maintenance training oversight by Air Midwest
Ineffective Continuing Analysis and Surveillance System (CASS) program implementation
Lack of detailed guidance for on-the-job training programs
Insufficient Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) oversight of air carrier maintenance training programs
recommendations: Require all Part 121 operators to revise their task documents and work cards to describe explicitly the process to be followed in accomplishing maintenance tasks.
Develop mandatory human factors programs to prevent human error in aviation maintenance
Ensure that air carrier on-the-job training programs are developed according to detailed guidance emphasizing effective practices
key_findings: The airplane entered the detail six maintenance check with an elevator control system rigged for full travel in the downward direction.
The incorrect rigging of the elevator control system restricted its travel to about one-half of the specified downward travel.
Air Midwest's weight and balance program was inaccurate, leading to an aft center of gravity that contributed to the loss of pitch control
FAA should aggressively pursue deficiencies in air carrier maintenance training programs

probable_cause: The captain's and first officer's failure to establish and maintain a proper glidepath during the night visual approach to landing.
contributing_factors: A combination of the captain's and first officer's fatigue
The captain's and first officer's failure to adhere to company flight procedures
The captain's and flight engineer's failure to monitor the approach
The first officer's color vision deficiency
safety_issues: Fatigue management in aviation industry
Effectiveness of current FAA-approved color vision test protocols
Need for a standard battery of tests to screen out applicants with color vision deficiencies that could impair their ability to perform critical aviation tasks
recommendations: Conduct research on the effectiveness of each of the current FAA-approved color vision test protocols at effectively screening out pilot applicants with color vision deficiencies

Develop a standard battery of tests to be performed at least once on each applicant for a Class 1 or 2 medical certificate that would prevent applicants with color vision deficiencies from being certificated without limitations
key_findings: The captain, first officer, and flight engineer possessed valid airman and medical certificates
The accident approach was not stabilized as the airplane descended through 500 feet above ground level
Black hole conditions likely contributed to the flight crew's failure to properly perform the approach

Precision approach path indicator lights were a recognized countermeasure for use in black hole conditions but were not effectively used by the first officer or monitored by the captain and flight engineer

probable_cause: the in-flight failure of the right engine and the pilot's failure to adequately manage the airplane's performance after the engine failed.
contributing_factors: inadequate maintenance that was performed by Air Sunshine's maintenance personnel during undocumented maintenance.
pilot's fail ure to provide an emergency briefing after the right engine failed.
safety_issues: maintenance record-keeping and practices were not adequate
Federal Aviation Administration’s oversight of Air Sunshine was insufficient to detect inadequate maintenance record-keeping and practices at the company
the pilot had a history of below-average flight proficiency
recommendations: Issue a flight standards information bulletin to principal operations inspectors of all Part 135 single-pilot operators that carry passengers and operate over water, which familiarizes them with the circumstances of the Air Sunshine flight 527 accident and emphasizes the need for pilots to provide timely emergency briefings.
key_findings: The airplane was properly certificated and equipped in accordance with Federal regulations.

Two or more of the right engine No. 2 cylinder hold-down nuts became loose and backed off of the studs, which resulted in the remaining studs and through bolts fracturing in high-stress fatigue, allowing the cylinder to separate from the engine.

The pilot was not wearing his shoulder harness during the ditching, and because he was not wearing his harness, he sustained a head injury that reduced his ability to assist passengers after the ditching.

probable_cause: Not directly provided in the text, but related to rapid airspeed changes and high forces applied to rudder pedals exceeding design limits.
contributing_factors: Rapid increase in airspeed during upset
High forces applied to rudder pedal when it was at the in-flight limit
safety_issues: Inadequate rudder travel limiter response to rapid airspeed changes
Maintenance manual inspection criteria not addressing all types of extreme accelerations
Need for a system to track and evaluate acceleration events exceeding manufacturer's threshold values based on FDR data
recommendations: Review and revise maintenance manual inspection criteria for severe turbulence and extreme in-flight maneuvers
Establish maximum threshold values for positive and negative vertical and lateral G accelerations beyond which direct manufacturer oversight is required
Require operators to notify the FAA of high loading events exceeding established thresholds based on FDR data
key_findings: Rudder travel limiter could not maintain designed rudder travel limitations during rapid airspeed changes experienced in flight upsets
Maintenance manuals did not include inspections for damage caused by extreme lateral accelerations or negative vertical accelerations
FDR analysis revealed that airplanes might be exceeding design and certification standards more frequently than previously known

probable_cause: The first officer's failure to properly apply crosswind landing techniques to align the airplane with the runway centerline and to properly arrest the airplane's descent rate before touchdown; and the captain's failure to adequately monitor the first officer's performance and command or initiate corrective action during the final approach and landing.
contributing_factors: First Officer's improper application of control wheel, rudder inputs, and back pressure on the control column
Captain's inadequate monitoring and lack of corrective action
safety_issues: FedEx's inadequate hands-on emergency procedures training
Differences between the trainer and door/slide installation on the accident airplane
Flight crewmembers delaying evacuation to salvage personal items
ARFF response vehicles unnecessarily delayed due to air traffic control
recommendations: Establish programs for flight crew members with performance deficiencies or training failures (A-05-014)
Amend emergency exit training information in FAA Order 8400.10 (A-05-015)
Verify accuracy of emergency door/slide trainers and hands-on training (A-05-016)
Inform air traffic control tower controllers about ARFF protocols (A-05-017)
Modify the Airport Emergency Procedures letter of agreement with Memphis Fire Department (A-05-018)
key_findings: Flight crew was properly certified and qualified
Fatigue was not a factor in this accident
The airplane was properly certificated, maintained, equipped, and dispatched
Cargo loading was not a factor
Handling characteristics differences between MD-11 and MD-10 were not an issue
Air traffic control was not a factor
Atmospheric conditions during approach and landing were within airplane's performance capabilities

probable_cause: The captain's failure to execute proper techniques to recover from the bounced landings and his subsequent failure to execute a go-around.
contributing_factors: Captain did not make appropriate pitch and power corrections after each bounce of the airplane on the runway
Flight crewmembers did not account for winds when calculating the minimum approach airspeed, resulting in non-compliance with Executive Airlines' approach airspeed procedures
The captain demonstrated poor cockpit oversight and piloting techniques before and during the accident sequence
safety_issues: Unreliable aileron flight control surface position sensors installed on airplanes in accordance with Supplemental Type Certificate No. ST01310NY
Lack of written company guidance on bounced landing recovery techniques
First officer's medical condition and prescription drug use
recommendations: Require all 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 121 and 135 air carriers to incorporate bounced landing recovery techniques in their flight manuals and teach these techniques during initial and recurrent training.

Replace aileron surface position sensors installed in accordance with Supplemental Type Certificate (STC) No. ST01310NY with more reliable ones within 1 year or at the next heavy maintenance check, whichever comes first, after issuance of an approved STC. Until then, require flight data recorder functional checks every 6 months and replacement of faulty sensors as necessary.

Conduct a review of all flight data recorder systems that have been modified by a supplemental type certificate to determine the reliability of all sensors used as flight control surface position sensors. If unreliable, require replacement with more reliable sensors.
key_findings: The captain was properly certificated and qualified under Federal regulations.
No evidence indicated any preexisting medical or physical conditions that might have adversely affected his performance during the accident flight.
The airplane was properly certificated, equipped, and maintained in accordance with Federal regulations and approved company procedures.
Winds were within the airplane's performance capabilities and did not adversely affect the flight crew's ability to maneuver the airplane during the approach and landing.
Emergency response was timely and appropriate; passengers and crewmembers were safely evacuated from the airplane.

flight_type: 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 121 flight operations
probable_cause: The pilots' failure to follow established procedures and properly conduct a nonprecision instrument approach at night in instrument meteorological conditions, including their descent below the minimum descent altitude (MDA) before required visual cues were available (which continued unmoderated until the airplane struck the trees) and their failure to adhere to the established division of duties between the flying and nonflying (monitoring) pilot.
contributing_factors: pilots' failure to make standard callouts
current Federal Aviation Regulations that allow pilots to descend below the MDA into a region in which safe obstacle clearance is not assured based upon seeing only the airport approach lights.
failure to establish and maintain a professional demeanor during the flight
fatigue
safety_issues: Sterile cockpit rule compliance
Nonprecision approach procedures
Minimum descent altitude regulations
Pilot fatigue management
Cockpit image recording systems
Flight data recorder requirements
recommendations: Direct principal operations inspectors to reemphasize the importance of strict compliance with the sterile cockpit rule.
Require operators to incorporate constant-angle-of-descent technique into nonprecision approach procedures.

Revise regulations to prohibit pilots from descending below MDA during nonprecision approaches unless conditions allow for clear visual identification of all obstacles and terrain along the approach path or vertical guidance is available and being used.
Modify flight crew hours-of-service regulations based on recent research, scientific evidence, and industry experience.
Require operators to incorporate fatigue-related information into pilot training programs.
key_findings: Pilots were properly certificated and qualified in accordance with Federal regulations and company requirements.
The accident airplane was properly certificated and maintained and equipped according to applicable regulations and industry practices.
Weather conditions made the nonprecision approach more challenging but did not cause the accident.
Corporate Airlines' policies, procedures, and training were consistent with industry standards.
Air traffic control was not a factor in the accident.
The pilots failed to follow established procedures for monitoring descent rate and height above terrain during the later stages of the approach.
Sterile cockpit regulations were violated by nonessential conversation below 10,000 feet mean sea level.

probable_cause: The flight crew's failure to identify and arrest the helicopter's descent for undetermined reasons, which resulted in controlled flight into terrain.
contributing_factors: Failure of the flight crew to adequately monitor the helicopter's altitude
Lack of a terrain awareness and warning system aboard the accident helicopter
safety_issues: Preflight testing and maintenance checks of an aircraft's cockpit voice recorder (CVR) are both necessary to ensure that audio data from CVR recordings are adequate.
Exemption of S-76A helicopters from flight data recorder requirements significantly hampers accident investigations.
recommendations: Require all existing and new U.S.-registered turbine-powered rotorcraft certificated for six or more passenger seats to be equipped with a terrain awareness and warning system.

Ensure that all operators of helicopters equipped with either the SPZ-7000 or SPZ-7600 dual digital automatic flight control systems provide training that includes information on flight director and coupling status annunciations; the command cue presentations when only the pitch or the roll mode is engaged; and, if applicable, the differences between the SPZ-7000 and the SPZ-7600.
Ensure that the infrastructure for the National Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast Program in the Gulf of Mexico is operational by fiscal year 2010.

Until the infrastructure for the National Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast Program in the Gulf of Mexico is fully operational, require principal operations inspectors of Gulf of Mexico aircraft operators to inform the operators about the benefits of commercial flight-tracking systems and encourage the operators to acquire such systems.

Require all operators of aircraft equipped with a cockpit voice recorder (CVR) to test the functionality of the CVR before the first flight of each day as part of an approved aircraft checklist and perform a periodic maintenance check of the CVR.
key_findings: The captain and copilot were properly certificated, qualified under Federal regulations, and no evidence indicated any medical or behavioral conditions that might have adversely affected their performance during the accident flight.
Weather was not a factor in this accident.
The helicopter crashed into the water at a high airspeed, shallow descent angle, and near-level roll attitude.
Pilots were not attempting an emergency landing on the water.

probable_cause: Fuel starvation resulting from the captain's decision not to follow approved fuel crossfeed procedures.
contributing_factors: Captain's inadequate preflight planning
Captain's subsequent distraction during the flight
Late initiation of the in-range checklist by the captain
Flight crew's failure to monitor the fuel gauges and recognize that the airplane's changing handling characteristics were caused by a fuel imbalance
safety_issues: Importance of following approved procedures for fuel crossfeed operations
Need for pilots to maintain situational awareness during critical flight phases
The benefit of cockpit voice recorder power source and cockpit image recorders in accident investigations
recommendations: Require retrofit after January 1, 2005, of all cockpit voice recorders (CVR) on all airplanes required to carry both a CVR and a flight data recorder with a CVR that meets Technical Standard Order (TSO) C123a, is capable of recording the last 2 hours of audio, and is fitted with an independent power source that automatically engages and provides 10 minutes of operation whenever aircraft power to the recorder ceases.
key_findings: The captain's preflight weight and balance calculations were incorrect but did not affect the flight's safety.
Fuel transfer occurred due to improper fuel crossfeed operations, leading to a fuel imbalance.
Dual-engine flameout resulted from the exhaustion of fuel in the left tank during descent.

flight_type: On-demand charter flight under 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 135
probable_cause: The pilots' failure to ensure the airplane was loaded within weight-and-balance limits and their attempt to take off with the center of gravity well forward of the forward takeoff limit, which prevented the airplane from rotating at the intended rotation speed.
contributing_factors: Platinum Jet Management's (PJM) conduct of charter flights without proper FAA certification and its failure to ensure that all for-hire flights were conducted in accordance with 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 135 requirements

Darby Aviation's failure to maintain operational control over 14 CFR Part 135 flights being conducted under its certificate by PJM, which resulted in an environment conducive to the development of systemic patterns of flight crew performance deficiencies like those observed in this accident
The failure of the Birmingham, Alabama, FAA Flight Standards District Office to provide adequate surveillance and oversight of operations conducted under Darby's Part 135 certificate
The FAA's tacit approval of arrangements such as that between Darby and PJM
safety_issues: Pilots' lack of proper training and certification for the flight
Improper modification of weight and balance forms by Platinum Jet Management pilots
Failure to ensure seatbelt compliance before takeoff
Inadequate FAA oversight of charter management agreements
recommendations: Disseminate guidance on maintaining operational control over all on-demand charter flights conducted under a certificate holder's authority, including operations based at locations geographically distant from the certificate holder's base.
Review and revise any charter management, lease, or other agreements that permit and/or enable a loss of operational control by the certificate holder.
Require 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 135 certificate holders to ensure seatbelts are visible and accessible before each flight.
Require basic FAA-approved safety training for cabin personnel on board 14 CFR Part 135 flights who could be perceived as equivalent to a qualified flight attendant.
key_findings: The captain had not received the required training needed to operate under 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 135.
The first officer was not properly trained or certificated for the accident flight.
There was no evidence that fatigue affected the pilots' performance on the morning of the accident.
Weather and aircraft mechanical issues were not factors in the accident.
Pilots failed to ensure the airplane's weight and center of gravity were within approved takeoff limits, which severely degraded the airplane's ability to rotate.

probable_cause: The accident airplane's aerodynamic stall and loss of control due to the flight crew's decision to climb to the maximum operating altitude at less than its best rate of climb speed, leading to a disruption in airflow to the engine inlets causing both engines to flame out.
contributing_factors: Flight crew's decision to climb to the airplane's maximum operating altitude
Slowing down to stall speed during ascent
safety_issues: Core lock condition that can preclude pilots from restarting an engine after a double engine failure
recommendations: Require manufacturers to perform high power, high altitude sudden engine shutdown tests and determine the minimum airspeed required for core rotation
Ensure airplane flight manuals clearly state the minimum airspeed required for core rotation and potential loss of in-flight restart capability due to core lock

Include significant performance penalties such as loss of glide distance and increased descent rate from maintaining minimum airspeed required for core rotation and windmill restart attempts in airplane flight manuals
Review turbine-powered engine designs to determine susceptibility to core lock and require manufacturers to perform high power, high altitude sudden engine shutdown tests

Incorporate information into airplane flight manuals about the potential for core lock, procedures including minimum airspeed required to prevent this condition from occurring after a sudden engine shutdown, and resulting loss of in-flight restart capability if this condition were to occur

Determine during 14 CFR Part 25 certification tests if restart capability exists from a core rotation speed of 0 indicated rpm after high power, high altitude sudden engine shutdowns and mitigate the hazard by providing design or operational means to ensure restart capability
Establish certification requirements that would place upper limits on the value of minimum airspeed required and amount of altitude loss permitted for windmill restarts
key_findings: The flight crew's decision to climb to the airplane's maximum operating altitude at less than its best rate of climb speed led to an aerodynamic stall
Both engines flamed out due to disrupted airflow during the upset, and core rotation speeds decreased until reaching 0 indicated rpm
Engine restart attempts were unsuccessful likely due to a condition known as 'core lock'

probable_cause: The flight crew's failure to effectively monitor and maintain airspeed and comply with procedures for deice boot activation on the approach, which caused an aerodynamic stall from which they did not recover.
contributing_factors: The Federal Aviation Administration's failure to establish adequate certification requirements for flight into icing conditions, which led to the inadequate stall warning margin provided by the airplane's stall warning system.
safety_issues: Pilot training and monitoring skills
Deice boot activation procedures in icing conditions
Automatic deice boot cycling mode
Stall warning system effectiveness in icing conditions
recommendations: Require operational training to emphasize the airplane flight manual requirements for airspeed increase and deice boot operation during approaches when ice is present on the wings.
Modify all pilot training programs to contain modules that teach monitoring skills, workload management, and opportunities to practice these areas.
Revise guidance in manuals and training programs to emphasize leading edge deice boots should be activated as soon as the airplane enters icing conditions.
Require pneumatic deice boot-equipped airplanes certified for known icing conditions to have an automatic mode incorporated into their deice boot system.
Review and test pneumatic deice boot-equipped airplanes against revised icing certification standards.
Modify the Cessna 560 airplane's stall warning system to provide a stall warning margin that accounts for ice size, type, and distribution.
key_findings: The flight crew did not increase the landing reference airspeed while operating in icing conditions contrary to company procedures.
The airplane encountered supercooled large droplet (SLD) conditions during about the last 4.5 minutes of the flight.
The flight crew failed to maintain adequate airspeed during the final approach in icing conditions, leading to an aerodynamic stall from which they did not recover.

flight_type: 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 91 nonstop sightseeing air tour flight
probable_cause: The pilot's decision to continue flight under visual flight rules into an area of turbulent, reduced visibility weather conditions, which resulted in the pilot's spatial disorientation and loss of control of the helicopter.
contributing_factors: pilot's inexperience in assessing local weather conditions
inadequate Federal Aviation Administration surveillance of Special Federal Aviation Regulation 71 operating restrictions
the operator's pilot-scheduling practices that likely had an adverse impact on pilot decision-making and performance
safety_issues: Pilot spatial disorientation due to deteriorating weather conditions
Inexperienced pilots' ability to make appropriate in-flight decisions when faced with deteriorating weather
Lack of cue-based training for local island climate conditions
Bali Hai's pilot-scheduling practices impacting decision-making and performance
Insufficient FAA resources for air tour surveillance
recommendations: Develop a cue-based training program for commercial air tour pilots in Hawaii that specifically addresses hazardous aspects of local weather phenomena and in-flight decision-making.
Require all commercial air tour operators in Hawaii to provide this training to newly hired pilots.
Establish operational practices for commercial air tour helicopter pilots that include rest breaks and ensure acceptable pilot performance and safety.
Develop a permanent mechanism to provide direct surveillance of commercial air tour operations in the State of Hawaii and enforce commercial air tour regulations.
Ensure annual safety meetings focus on pertinent and timely commercial air tour safety issues
Reevaluate altitude restrictions in the state of Hawaii
Develop and enforce safety standards for all commercial air tour operations
Accelerate implementation of ADS-B infrastructure in Hawaii to include high-quality services to low-flying aircraft along heavily traveled commercial air tour routes.
Require Hawaii air tour operators equip tour aircraft with compatible ADS-B technology within 1 year of the installation of a functional National ADS-B Program infrastructure in Hawaii.
key_findings: The pilot was properly certificated and qualified under Federal regulations to conduct the flight
No evidence indicated any preexisting medical condition that might have adversely affected his performance during the flight.
There was no evidence that powerplant, system, or structural failures of the helicopter contributed to the accident.
Bali Hai's pilot-scheduling practices likely had an adverse impact on pilot decision-making and performance.
The FAA has not provided sufficient resources for the Honolulu Flight Standards District Office to implement air tour surveillance.

probable_cause: the in-flight failure and separation of the right wing during normal flight, which resulted from (1) the failure of the Chalk's Ocean Airways maintenance program to identify and properly repair fatigue cracks in the right wing and (2) the failure of the Federal Aviation Administration to detect and correct deficiencies in the company's maintenance program.
contributing_factors: failure of the Chalk's Ocean Airways maintenance program to identify and properly repair fatigue cracks in the right wing
failure of the FAA to detect and correct deficiencies in the company's maintenance program
safety_issues: air carrier maintenance programs and practices
FAA oversight procedures for air carrier maintenance programs
recommendations: Verify that the maintenance programs of commercial aircraft operators include stringent criteria to address recurring or systemic discrepancies to include, if necessary, further analysis of the discrepancies through a comprehensive engineering evaluation.

Identify the systemic deficiencies in the maintenance program oversight procedures that led to this accident and modify those procedures to ensure that the maintenance program plans for commercial operators are adequate to ensure the continued airworthiness, both structural and otherwise, of the operator's fleet.
key_findings: The captain and first officer were properly certificated and qualified under Federal regulations.
The accident airplane was certificated and equipped in accordance with Federal regulations but showed evidence of multiple failures of wing structural components.
Chalk's Ocean Airways' maintenance program plan was inadequate to maintain the structural integrity of its aircraft fleet.

probable_cause: The flight crew's failure to adequately monitor the airplane's position during taxi and their failure to cross-check and confirm that they were on the correct runway before takeoff, which resulted in a takeoff from the wrong runway.
contributing_factors: Nonpertinent conversation by the flight crewmembers during taxi
Influence of confirmation bias
safety_issues: Runway incursion prevention
Pilot positional awareness and standard operating procedures compliance
Controller oversight in monitoring aircraft movements on the ground
recommendations: Require all air traffic controllers to complete instructor-led initial and recurrent training in resource management skills that will improve controller judgment, vigilance, and safety awareness.
Develop and implement a fatigue awareness and countermeasures training program for all Air Traffic Organization operational service units.
key_findings: The flight crew believed they were on runway 22 when they initiated the takeoff roll from runway 26
Adequate cues existed to navigate successfully to runway 22, but these were not utilized effectively by the flight crew
Nonpertinent conversation during taxi likely contributed to loss of positional awareness

location: WN Chicago Midway International Airport Chicago, Illinois
probable_cause: The flight crew's failure to properly assess and account for the effects of runway contamination on aircraft performance, which resulted in an inadequate landing distance assessment and a subsequent overrun during the landing roll.
contributing_factors: The flight crew's lack of familiarity with the specific characteristics of the contaminated runway at Chicago Midway International Airport
Inadequate guidance provided by the airline to its pilots regarding operations on contaminated runways
safety_issues: Lack of standardized criteria for pilot braking assessments and reports
Insufficient runway safety areas (RSAs) that meet current standards at many airports
The absence of an operationally meaningful tool to convert runway friction index into a measure useful for pilots and air carriers
recommendations: Require all Part 139 certificated airports to upgrade RSAs to meet minimum standards whenever feasible
Expand research efforts to develop tools that correlate friction measurements with aircraft stopping performance
Convene an industry-government group to develop standardized criteria for pilot braking assessments and guidance
key_findings: The flight crew did not perform a proper landing distance assessment considering the contaminated runway conditions
Runway safety areas at Chicago Midway International Airport were inadequate, contributing to the severity of the overrun incident
There was no operationally meaningful tool available for pilots to assess braking performance on contaminated runways

operator: United Parcel Service Company Flight 1307 McDonnell Douglas DC
probable_cause: An in-flight cargo fire that initiated from an unknown source, which was most likely located within cargo container 12, 13, or 14.

Lack of an on-board fire suppression system
safety_issues: Adequacy of guidance to flight crews in responding to evidence of a fire
Performance requirements for smoke and fire detection systems
Need for fire suppression systems in cargo compartments
Training methods for aircraft rescue and firefighting personnel
Cargo aircraft inclusion in airport's aircraft familiarization training programs
Designation of floor level emergency exits on cargo aircraft
Provision of contrasting colored bands outlining exit doors
Measures to reduce the risk of primary lithium batteries becoming involved in fires
Transportation requirements for secondary lithium batteries
Immediate provision of hazardous materials information to emergency responders
Reporting and analysis of incidents involving lithium batteries
recommendations: Provide clear guidance on flight crew procedures for responding to evidence of a fire (A-07-97)
Ensure performance requirements for smoke and fire detection systems account for cargo containers (A-07-98)
Require installation of fire suppression systems in cargo compartments (A-07-99)
Provide guidance on best training methods for high-reach extendable turret proficiency (A-07-100)
Ensure Part 139 airports include cargo aircraft in ARFF familiarization programs (A-07-101)
Designate floor level emergency exits and equip with evacuation slides when appropriate (A-07-102)
Require contrasting colored bands outlining exit doors on cargo aircraft (A-07-103)
Implement measures to reduce the risk of primary lithium batteries becoming involved in fires (A-07-104)
Transport secondary lithium batteries in crew-accessible locations until fire suppression systems are required (A-07-105)
Require immediate provision of hazardous materials information to emergency responders upon notification of an accident or incident (A-07-106)
Report all incidents involving primary and secondary lithium batteries to PHMSA for evaluation purposes (A-07-107)
Analyze the causes of thermal failures and fires involving lithium batteries and take appropriate action (A-07-108)
Eliminate regulatory exemptions for packaging, marking, and labeling of small secondary lithium batteries until analysis is completed (A-07-109)
Develop and disseminate accurate and complete airplane Emergency Response diagrams for ARFF personnel at airports with cargo operations (A-07-110)
key_findings: The fire most likely originated from within one of the cargo containers
Certification test requirements for smoke and fire detection systems were inadequate
Lack of an on-board fire suppression system contributed to the loss of the aircraft
Guidance on flight crew procedures in responding to evidence of a fire was needed
Training methods for ARFF personnel with high-reach extendable turret proficiency required improvement
Cargo aircraft inclusion in airport's familiarization training programs was lacking
Designation and equipment of floor level emergency exits were inadequate
Measures to reduce the risk of lithium batteries causing fires on cargo-only aircraft needed implementation
Transportation requirements for secondary lithium batteries needed adjustment until fire suppression systems are required
Immediate provision of hazardous materials information to emergency responders was not provided in a timely manner
Reporting and analysis of incidents involving lithium batteries were incomplete

probable_cause: The failure of the flight crew to execute a missed approach when visual cues for the runway were not distinct and identifiable.
contributing_factors: The crew's decision to descend to the instrument landing system decision height instead of the localizer (glideslope out) minimum descent altitude
The first officer's long landing on a short contaminated runway and the crew's failure to use reverse thrust and braking to their maximum effectiveness
The captain's fatigue, which affected his ability to effectively plan for and monitor the approach and landing
Shuttle America's failure to administer an attendance policy that permitted flight crewmembers to call in as fatigued without fear of reprisals
safety_issues: Pilot decision-making during low-visibility conditions
Fatigue management policies for flight crews
Training on rejected landings and maximum performance landings on contaminated runways
Go-around callout procedures and crew response
recommendations: Require Part 121, 135, and Part 91 subpart K operators to include decision-making for rejected landings below 50 feet along with a rapid reduction in visual cues and practice in executing this maneuver
Require Part 121, 135, and Part 91 subpart K operators to include practice for pilots in accomplishing maximum performance landings on contaminated runways
Require Part 121, 135, and Part 91 subpart K operators to have a written policy emphasizing that either pilot can make a go-around callout and the response is an immediate missed approach
Develop a specific, standardized policy for flight crewmembers to decline assignments or remove themselves from duty if they are impaired by lack of sleep
Require Part 121, 135, and Part 91 subpart K operators to adopt this fatigue policy and provide details in writing to their flight crewmembers
key_findings: The captain and first officer were properly certified and qualified under Federal regulations.
The accident airplane was properly certificated, equipped, and maintained according to Federal regulations.
Weather conditions had deteriorated with moderate to heavy snow reported during the approach and at landing time.
Controllers performed their duties properly and provided timely weather and runway condition information.
Airport personnel appropriately monitored runway conditions and provided snow removal services.

probable_cause: The pilots' decision to land at Cherry Capital Airport (TVC), Traverse City, Michigan, without performing a landing distance assessment, which was required by company policy because of runway contamination initially reported by TVC ground operations personnel and continuing reports of deteriorating weather and runway conditions during the approach.
contributing_factors: The Federal Aviation Administration pilot flight and duty time regulations that permitted the pilots' long, demanding duty day
The TVC operations supervisor's use of ambiguous and unspecific radio phraseology in providing runway braking information
safety_issues: Pilot fatigue due to long and demanding duty days
Ambiguous communication regarding runway conditions
Lack of landing distance assessment before landing on contaminated runways
Need for improved crash detection and location technologies
recommendations: Emphasize with principal operations inspectors the importance of conducting timely postaccident drug and alcohol testing.
Address the need for initial training on the rationale for and criticality of conducting landing distance assessments before landing on contaminated runways.

Issue a CertAlert to all Part 139 certificated airports that describes the circumstances of this accident, emphasizes the importance of specific and decisive radio communications, and urges airports to ensure those criteria are being met in all airfield radio communications.

Require all Part 139 certificated airport operators to include absolute criteria for type and depth of contamination and runway friction assessments in their snow and ice control plan that would trigger immediate closure of the affected runway to air carrier operations.
key_findings: The pilots were properly certified and qualified under Federal regulations.
No evidence indicated any medical conditions that might have adversely affected the pilots' performance during the accident flight.
Cherry Capital Airport's snow removal operations and runway surface condition assessment activities were conducted in accordance with the airport's FAA-approved plan.
Pinnacle Airlines failed to perform required postaccident alcohol tests, which prevents a definitive statement on the issue of alcohol use.

operator:
United States Parachute Association regarding this issue.

The  National  Transportation  Safety  Board  is  an  independent  Federal  agency  dedicated  to  promoting  aviation,  railroad, highway, marine, pipeline, and hazardous materials safety. Established in 1967, the agency is mandated by Congress through the Independent Safety Board Act of 1974 to investigate transportation accidents, determine the probable causes of the accidents, issue safety recommendations, study transportation safety issues, and evaluate the safety effectiveness of government agencies involved in transportation. The Safety Board makes public its actions and decisions through accident reports, safety studies, special investigation reports, safety recommendations, and statistical reviews.

Recent publications are available in their entirety on the Web at
probable_cause: the pilot's failure to maintain airspeed following a loss of power in the right engine due to the fracturing of compressor turbine blades for undetermined reasons.
contributing_factors: lack of a more effective restraint system on the airplane
safety_issues: inadequate protection provided by single-point restraints for parachutists
recommendations: Conduct research, in conjunction with the United States Parachute Association, to determine the most effective dual-point restraint systems for parachutists that reflects the various aircraft and seating configurations used in parachute operations.

Work with the Federal Aviation Administration to conduct research to determine the most effective dual-point restraint systems for parachutists that reflects the various aircraft and seating configurations used in parachute operations.
key_findings: Although damage to the accident airplane's right engine precluded termination of the initial event that precipitated the overload fracturing of the compressor turbine blades, it is possible that the initiating fracture event within the engine resulted from a condition that could have been detected and corrected during an engine overhaul performed within the manufacturer's recommended time between overhauls.

The pilot's failure to maintain airspeed according to the technique specified in the published emergency procedures following the loss of power in one engine negated any possibility of continued, controlled flight that could have allowed for a return to the airport or other suitable landing area.

flight_type: personal flight under the provisions of 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 91 on an instrument flight rules flight plan
probable_cause: the actions and decisions by NASCAR's corporate aviation division's management and maintenance personnel to allow the accident airplane to be released for flight with a known and unresolved discrepancy, and the accident pilots' decision to operate the airplane with that known discrepancy, a discrepancy that likely resulted in an in-flight fire
contributing_factors: known unresolved maintenance discrepancy
decision by pilots to fly despite known discrepancy
safety_issues: resetting of circuit breakers
inspection and maintenance of electrical systems in general aviation aircraft
establishment of Safety Management Systems in general aviation corporate operations
recommendations: Develop a safety alert for operators informing general aviation pilots and maintenance personnel of the circuit breaker policy contained in Advisory Circular 120-80.
Require that the contents of the safety alert be included in initial and required biennial training for general aviation pilots and maintenance personnel.
Require aircraft manufacturers to improve existing guidance regarding which circuit breakers pilots should and should not attempt to reset before or during flight.

Require initial and recurrent training for maintenance personnel working on general aviation aircraft include current best practices regarding inspection and maintenance of electrical systems, circuit breakers, and aging wiring.
Develop a safety alert encouraging all 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 91 business operators to adopt Safety Management System programs that include sound risk management practices.
key_findings: weather radar system anomaly could have developed into significant in-flight smoke and fire event
pilots accepted the airplane despite no diagnostic, corrective or interim maintenance action taken for the discrepancy
insufficient evidence to conclusively determine origin of in-flight fire

probable_cause: Both pilots' failure to see and avoid the other helicopter.
contributing_factors: Pilots' responsibility to perform reporting and visual tracking duties for their station's ENG operation.
Lack of formal procedures for Phoenix-area ENG pilots regarding conduct of these operations.
safety_issues: Limitations of the see-and-avoid concept during high-density traffic operations, especially when the pilot is conducting other non-flying duties as part of the operation.
Need for improved helicopter visibility through high-visibility paint schemes and anticollision lights.
Necessity of electronic traffic advisory systems to alert pilots to nearby aircraft.
recommendations: Require ENG operators to assign reporting responsibilities to someone other than the flying pilot unless it can be determined that the pilot's workload would remain manageable under all conditions.
Require ENG operators to use high-visibility blade paint schemes and high-visibility anticollision lights on their aircraft.
Develop standards for helicopter cockpit electronic traffic advisory systems so pilots are alerted to nearby aircraft.
Once standards for helicopter cockpit electronic traffic advisory systems are developed, require ENG operators to install this equipment on their aircraft.
Host annual regional conferences with local ENG helicopter and Federal Aviation Administration personnel to discuss operational and safety issues.
Develop or amend Letters of Agreement (LOAs) based on identified safety issues from the regional conferences to specify minimum horizontal and vertical aircraft separation requirements.
Incorporate pertinent information from Helicopter Association International's ENG Aviation Safety Manual into an advisory circular detailing best practices for ENG operations.
Require installation of crash-resistant flight recorder systems on newly manufactured turbine-powered, non-experimental, non-restricted-category aircraft operating under 14 CFR Parts 91, 121, or 135.
Retrofit existing turbine-powered, non-experimental, non-restricted-category aircraft with crash-resistant flight recorder systems.
key_findings: Pilots were properly certificated and qualified in accordance with applicable Federal regulations.
Helicopters were properly certified, equipped, and maintained according to Federal regulations.
Recovered components showed no evidence of pre-impact structural, engine, or system failures.
Weather was not a factor in the accident; sun glare would not have interfered with pilots' ability to detect other helicopters over the pursuit scene.
The channel 15 pilot's color vision deficiency was not a factor in this accident.

probable_cause: American Airlines' maintenance personnel's use of an inappropriate manual engine-start procedure, which led to the uncommanded opening of the left engine air turbine starter valve, and a subsequent left engine fire, which was prolonged by the flight crew's interruption of an emergency checklist to perform nonessential tasks.
contributing_factors: Deficiencies in American Airlines' Continuing Analysis and Surveillance System program
safety_issues: Maintenance personnel using unapproved procedures
Inadequate inspection criteria for air turbine starter valve-air filter
Pilot training methods for responding to multiple systems failures, competing task demands, and increased workload
Cockpit atmosphere affecting pilots' responses during emergencies
recommendations: Improved pilot training methods for responding to multiple systems failures, competing task demands, and increased workload.
Operational procedures requiring that an airplane be configured for an evacuation when it is stopped away from the gate after a significant event.
key_findings: Maintenance personnel did not clean the air turbine starter valve-air filter in accordance with its C check cleaning procedures
The filter element of the air turbine starter valve-air filter disintegrated, allowing the end cap to become free and block airflow
Pilots failed to properly allocate tasks and manage workload during emergency situation
Cockpit atmosphere before takeoff affected pilots' responses during emergencies

probable_cause: insufficient positive separation between electrical wiring and oxygen system components resulting in a contact between electrical wiring and an element of the supplemental oxygen system, causing electrically induced heat in a conductive oxygen hose, failure and ignition of the hose, allowing fire to initiate in the supernumerary compartment and rapidly spread
contributing_factors: Federal Aviation Administration's (FAA) failure to require replacement of conductive oxygen hoses after safety issue was initially identified by Boeing
safety_issues: Proximity between electrical wiring and oxygen system components
Use of combustible, electrically conductive oxygen hoses
Potential for passenger service unit reading lights with exposed electrical contacts to create inadvertent short circuits
Lack of smoke detectors in supernumerary compartments
recommendations: Require replacement/installation of non-conductive oxygen hoses
Implement effective method of electrically grounding the supplemental oxygen system to airframe
Update standards for installation and inspection procedures regarding separation between wiring and oxygen system components
Provide guidance on best training methods for aircraft rescue and firefighting personnel using high-reach extendable turret with skin-penetrating nozzle
key_findings: Fire began when electrically conductive oxygen hose became energized by short circuit, igniting and burning through
Oxygen leaks are a safety hazard as they can facilitate fires
Aging polyvinyl chloride flexible hoses more likely to leak oxygen due to degradation
Excessive number of reported discrepancies regarding the accident airplane's oxygen system indicative of chronic problem

location: Flight Collision with Large Birds Oklahoma City, Oklahoma
flight_type: Noncommercial operations as a single pilot, but operating contrary to its Federal Aviation Administration-issued Part 135 operating certificate
probable_cause: Airplane wing-structure damage sustained during impact with one or more large birds (American white pelicans), which resulted in a loss of control of the airplane.
contributing_factors: Wiley Post Airport's failure to perform wildlife hazard assessments as required, preventing determination of mitigation measures that could have been implemented to reduce bird strike risk
The second pilot was not trained or qualified to fly the accident airplane, although this did not contribute directly to the loss of control due to wing-structure damage from bird strikes
safety_issues: Inconsistent protection from in-flight impact with birds across all airframe structures under current certification requirements
Lack of compliance by federally obligated general aviation airports near wildlife attractants in performing required wildlife hazard assessments
Insufficient guidance for pilots to devise precautionary aircraft operational strategies for minimizing bird-strike damage severity
Low level of participation in voluntary wildlife strike reporting, leading to incomplete data for developing risk management and mitigation programs
Lack of a requirement for all operators to report wildlife strikes to the FAA National Wildlife Strike Database
Exclusion of an expanding class of turbine-powered airplanes from 14 CFR Part 91.23 truth-in-leasing regulations
Absence of a flight plan block for pilots to identify the operator and specify operating rules under which the flight is conducted
Lack of requirement for 14 CFR Part 135 on-demand operators to provide customers with written terms of carriage including regulatory part information
Need for an updated aircraft charter guide on FAA's website with reliable certification status information
Insufficient emphasis and strategies in on-site inspector surveillance activities at airports and flight operations to detect improper charter operations
recommendations: Revise bird-strike certification requirements for Part 25 airplanes so that protection from in-flight impact with birds is consistent across all airframe structures, considering current database information and trends
Verify compliance of federally obligated general aviation airports near wildlife attractants to perform required wildlife hazard assessments
Require aircraft manufacturers to develop guidance for pilots on devising precautionary operational strategies for minimizing bird-strike damage severity
Require all Part 139 airports and Part 121, 135, and 91K operators to report all wildlife strikes to the FAA National Wildlife Strike Database
Revise truth-in-leasing regulations to include all turbine-powered airplanes
Require flight plans to include a block for pilots to identify operator and specify operating rules under which the flight is conducted
Require Part 135 on-demand operators to provide customers with written terms of carriage including regulatory part information
Update FAA website aircraft charter guide with reliable certification status information
Explore and implement strategies to improve on-site inspector surveillance activities at airports and flight operations to detect improper charter operations
Assess why existing policies, procedures, and practices failed to detect noncompliant actions by Interstate Helicopters and develop additional methods for detecting noncompliant charter operations
key_findings: The accident pilot was certificated, trained, and qualified to fly the airplane in noncommercial operations as a single pilot
Interstate Helicopters was operating contrary to its FAA-issued Part 135 operating certificate by conducting fixed-wing aircraft operations without authorization
Neither the pilot nor second pilot were trained or qualified to fly the accident airplane in any commercial charter operation, and the airplane was not maintained according to Part 135 requirements
Interstate Helicopters labeled invoices as 'aircraft lease' and 'sales demo' flights to disguise noncompliant charter operations
The absence of written lease and FAA notification requirements for airplanes that do not meet the definition of 'large' increases likelihood of improper charters and hinders FAA detection

operator: ata recorders, autopilot panel design, control yoke wiring installations, identification of circuit breakers for use  in  emergencies,  aural  and  visual  alerts  to  pitch  trim
probable_cause: The pilots' mismanagement of an abnormal flight control situation through improper actions, including failing to control airspeed and to prioritize control of the airplane, and lack of crew coordination.
contributing_factors: Marlin Air's operational safety deficiencies, including the inadequate checkrides administered by Marlin Air's chief pilot/check airman
The Federal Aviation Administration's failure to detect and correct those deficiencies
safety_issues: Design and location of the yaw damper and autopilot switches on Cessna Citation series airplanes do not adequately protect against inadvertent activation of a system, which could have disastrous consequences.

A rounded type of sheathed wire bundle would fit better and be better protected within the control column shaft than the currently installed flat ribbon cable; replacement of the flat ribbon cable with a rounded type of sheathed wire bundle could result in fewer short circuits and other electrical events.

The incorporation of an aural pitch trim-in-motion warning and contrasting color bands on the pitch trim wheel in all Cessna Citation series airplanes would help pilots of those airplanes to more promptly recognize and correct runaway pitch trim situations before control forces become unmanageable.
recommendations: Amend the advisory materials associated with 14 Code of Federal Regulations 25.1309 to include consideration of structural failures and human/airplane system interaction failures in the assessment of safety-critical systems.

Adopt Society of Automotive Engineers [Aerospace Recommended Practice] 5150 into 14 Code of Federal Regulations Parts 21, 25, 33, and 121 to require a program for the monitoring and ongoing assessment of safety-critical systems throughout the life cycle of the airplane.
key_findings: The accident sequence initiated as a result of a control problem that was related to either an inadvertent autopilot activation or a pitch trim anomaly, the effects of which were compounded by aileron and/or rudder trim inputs.

Pilots would benefit from training and readily accessible guidance indicating that, when confronted with abnormal flight control forces, they should prioritize airplane control (airspeed, attitude, and configuration) before attempting to identify and eliminate the cause of the flight control problem.

probable_cause: The pilot's attempt to regain visual conditions by performing a rapid descent and his failure to arrest the descent at the minimum descent altitude during a nonprecision approach.
contributing_factors: the pilot's limited recent instrument flight experience
the lack of adherence to effective risk management procedures by the Maryland State Police
the pilot's inadequate assessment of the weather, which led to his decision to accept the flight
the failure of the Potomac Consolidated Terminal Radar Approach Control (PCT) controller to provide the current Andrews Air Force Base weather observation to the pilot
the increased workload on the pilot due to inadequate Federal Aviation Administration air traffic control handling by the Ronald Reagan National Airport Tower and PCT controllers
safety_issues: Lack of formal flight risk evaluation program at Maryland State Police before the accident.
Pilot's decision-making influenced by fatigue, late hour, and sleep apnea risk factors.
Inadequate instrument training program for pilots about 10 months before the accident.
Absence of a terrain awareness and warning system in the helicopter.
Procedural deficiencies in air traffic services provided to the flight.
recommendations: Require all emergency medical services (EMS) operators to develop and implement flight risk evaluation programs that include training all employees involved in the operation, procedures that support the systematic evaluation of flight risks, and consultation with others trained in EMS flight operations if the risks reach a predefined level.

Require all rotorcraft operating under 14 Code of Federal Regulations Parts 91 and 135 with a transport-category certification to be equipped with a cockpit voice recorder (CVR) and a flight data recorder (FDR). For those transport-category rotorcraft manufactured before October 11, 1991, require a CVR and an FDR or an onboard cockpit image recorder with the capability of recording cockpit audio, crew communications, and aircraft parametric data.

Require all emergency medical services (EMS) operators to install terrain awareness and warning systems on their aircraft and to provide adequate training to ensure that flight crews are capable of using the systems to safely conduct EMS operations.
key_findings: The pilot was properly certificated and qualified in accordance with Maryland State Police standards.
Instrument meteorological conditions prevailed in the accident area.
Had a formal flight risk evaluation program been in place at Maryland State Police before the accident, it may have resulted in the cancellation of the flight.
No evidence was found that suggests that the glideslope was not functioning properly.
The pilot's workload increased substantially and unexpectedly as a result of encountering instrument weather conditions.

flight_type: Commercial (14 Code of Federal Regulations Parts 121, 135, and 91K)

Low-airspeed alert systems installation

Professionalism in aircraft operations and sterile cockpit adherence

Simulator model fidelity requirements for stall recovery training
recommendations: Review standard operating procedures to verify consistency with flight crew monitoring techniques described in Advisory Circular (AC) 120-71A

Require airspeed indicator display systems on all aircraft certified under Part 25 and equipped with electronic flight instrument systems to depict a yellow/amber cautionary band above the low-speed cue or digits that change from white to yellow/amber as the airspeed approaches the low-speed cue

Install low-airspeed alert systems providing pilots with redundant aural and visual warnings of an impending hazardous low-speed condition on all airplanes engaged in commercial operations under Parts 121, 135, and 91K
Issue guidance on leadership training for upgrading captains at Part 121, 135, and 91K operators
Require specific course on leadership training to upgrading captains consistent with the advisory circular requested in Safety Recommendation A-10-13

Develop multimedia guidance materials on professionalism in aircraft operations that contain standards of performance for professionalism; best practices for sterile cockpit adherence; techniques for assessing and correcting pilot deviations; examples and scenarios; and a detailed review of accidents involving breakdowns in sterile cockpit and other procedures, including this accident
Require operators to address fatigue risks associated with commuting pilots

Document and retain electronic and/or paper records of pilot training and checking events in sufficient detail so that the carrier and its principal operations inspector can fully assess a pilot's entire training performance
Include the training records requested in Safety Recommendation A-10-17 as part of the remedial training program requested in Safety Recommendation A-05-14
Require operators to provide the training records requested in Safety Recommendation A-10-17 to hiring employers to fulfill their requirement under the Pilot Records Improvement Act
Develop a process for verifying, validating, auditing, and amending pilot training records at Part 121, 135, and 91K operators to guarantee the accuracy and completeness of the records

Direct operators of airplanes equipped with a reference speeds switch or similar device to develop procedures to establish that airspeed reference bugs are always matched to the position of the switch during approach and landing and implement specific training to ensure pilots demonstrate proficiency in this area

Require operators and Part 142 training centers to develop and conduct training that incorporates stalls that are fully developed, unexpected, involve autopilot disengagement, and include airplane-specific features such as a reference speeds switch
Require all operators of stick pusher-equipped aircraft to provide their pilots with pusher familiarization simulator training
Define and codify minimum simulator model fidelity requirements to support an expanded set of stall recovery training requirements
key_findings: The captain's inappropriate response to the activation of the stick shaker led to an aerodynamic stall from which the airplane did not recover.

Captain failed to effectively manage the flight, and Colgan Air had inadequate procedures for airspeed selection and management during approaches in icing conditions
The captain's inappropriate response to the stick shaker activation led to an aerodynamic stall from which recovery was not possible

the flight crew's poor crew resource management

flight crew's poor crew resource management skills

Identify deficiencies in Learjet's system safety analyses and require a comprehensive reassessment
Revise FAA guidance and oversight procedures to ensure effective identification and mitigation of hazards for all phases of flight
Apply current airworthiness regulations to derivative design aircraft certificated as changed aeronautical products

Define minimum simulator model fidelity requirements for tire failure scenarios during takeoff

Require a minimum level of flight time in an aircraft type for pilots flying in Part 135 operations to obtain consolidation of knowledge and skills
