Revise TSO-C69a to include quick-release girts and handholds on emergency evacuation slides
key_findings: The crew's actions were appropriate given the circumstances, including their decision to ditch in the Hudson River.
Passengers' survival was aided by the proximity of rescue services and the use of life preservers.
Current regulations for overwater operations and emergency equipment were found lacking.

probable_cause: The captain's cessation of right rudder input, which was needed to maintain directional control of the airplane, about 4 seconds before the excursion, when the airplane encountered a strong and gusty crosswind that exceeded the captain's training and experience.
contributing_factors: An air traffic control system that did not require or facilitate the dissemination of key, available wind information to the air traffic controllers and pilots
Inadequate crosswind training in the airline industry due to deficient simulator wind gust modeling
safety_issues: Air Traffic Control System Dissemination of Wind Information
Crosswind Training Deficiencies
Mountain Wave and Downslope Conditions at Airports Downwind of Mountainous Terrain
LLWAS Data Availability for Research
Documentation Compliance for LLWAS-Equipped Towers
Runway Selection Programs Considering Crosswinds
Realistic Gusty Crosswind Profiles in Simulator Training
recommendations: Conduct research into and document the effects of mountain wave and downslope conditions at airports downwind of mountainous terrain.
Archive all LLWAS data obtained from Denver International Airport and other similar airports for additional research.
Modify FAA Order 7110.65 to require air traffic controllers to provide pilots with maximum wind component, including gusts.
Review documentation compliance for LLWAS-equipped towers.
Require ATC towers to develop written runway selection programs considering crosswinds.
Gather data on surface winds at major U.S. airports and use these data to develop realistic, gusty crosswind profiles for pilot simulator training.
Incorporate realistic, gusty crosswind profiles into pilot simulator training programs.
Develop a standard methodology including pilot-in-the-loop testing for transport-category airplane manufacturers to establish empirically based, type-specific maximum-gusting-crosswind limitations.
key_findings: The captain's use of tiller and full right control wheel likely resulted from acute stress stemming from sudden unexpected threat.
Significant difference between the winds reported by DEN ATIS broadcast and provided to pilots was due to timing, sensor placement, and local wind field variations caused by mountain wave winds.
Denver International Airport air traffic control tower local controller did not provide information about most adverse crosswind conditions displayed on his ribbon display terminal.
LLWAS data could have helped pilots make more informed decisions if they were familiar with its content.

probable_cause: (1) the inherent limitations of the see-and-avoid concept, which made it difficult for the airplane pilot to see the helicopter until the final seconds before the collision, and (2) the Teterboro Airport local controller's nonpertinent telephone conversation, which distracted him from his air traffic control duties, including correcting the airplane pilot's read back of the Newark Liberty International Airport tower frequency and the timely transfer of communications for the accident airplane to the EWR tower.
contributing_factors: (1) both pilots' ineffective use of available electronic traffic information to maintain awareness of nearby aircraft
(2) inadequate Federal Aviation Administration procedures for transfer of communications among ATC facilities near the Hudson River Class B exclusion area
(3) FAA regulations that did not provide adequate vertical separation for aircraft operating in the Hudson River Class B exclusion area
safety_issues: Inherent limitations of the see-and-avoid concept
Distractions to air traffic controllers from nonpertinent telephone conversations
Pilots' ineffective use of electronic traffic information
Lack of adequate vertical separation for aircraft operating in the Hudson River special flight rules area
recommendations: Redefine boundaries of East River CTAF so Downtown Manhattan Heliport will be located in the area that uses the Hudson River CTAF.
Revise regulations to specify altitudes of use for aircraft conducting local operations in the Hudson River SFRA and incorporate altitude information onto VFR aeronautical charts.

Update Advisory Circular 90-48C to reflect current-day operations, including airspace classifications, air tour operational areas as high-volume traffic environments, and guidance on the use of electronic traffic advisory systems for pilots operating under the see-and-avoid concept.

Develop standards for helicopter cockpit electronic traffic advisory systems that address flight characteristics, reduce nuisance alerts, and consider different types of operations conducted by helicopters.
Require operators of helicopters used for passenger revenue flights to install electronic traffic advisory systems on their aircraft.
key_findings: The inherent limitations of the see-and-avoid concept made it difficult for the airplane pilot to detect the helicopter until the final seconds before collision
Distractions from nonpertinent telephone conversations impaired air traffic controllers' ability to perform duties effectively
Pilots did not use available electronic traffic information effectively, which could have helped maintain awareness of nearby aircraft
FAA procedures for transfer of communications among ATC facilities near the Hudson River Class B exclusion area were inadequate
Current FAA regulations do not provide adequate vertical separation for aircraft operating in the Hudson River special flight rules area

probable_cause: The intentional understatement of the helicopter's empty weight, alteration of the power available chart to exaggerate the helicopter's lift capability, and the practice of using unapproved above-minimum specification torque in performance calculations that collectively resulted in pilots relying on performance calculations significantly overestimating the helicopter's load-carrying capacity without an adequate performance margin for a successful takeoff; insufficient oversight by the U.S. Forest Service and the Federal Aviation Administration.
contributing_factors: Failure of flight crewmembers to address the fact that the helicopter had approached its maximum performance capability on their two prior departures from the accident site because they were accustomed to operating at the limit of the helicopter's performance
Immediate, intense fire resulting from fuel spillage upon impact due to non-crash resistant fuel tanks
Separation of cabin seats from floor during crash due to lack of crash-resistant design
Use of inappropriate release mechanism on cabin seat restraints
safety_issues: Inadequate oversight by the U.S. Forest Service and Federal Aviation Administration over Carson Helicopters, Inc.
Lack of accurate performance calculations for helicopter lift capability
Non-compliance with safety standards regarding fuel tanks, passenger seats, and restraint systems in S-61 helicopters
recommendations: Require hover performance charts to reflect true helicopter performance under all applicable conditions
Develop a surveillance program specifically for Part 135 operators of public aircraft
Clarify FAA authority over public aircraft oversight responsibilities
Require crash-resistant fuel tanks on S-61 helicopters used for passenger transport
Equip S-61 helicopters with improved passenger seats and seat mounting structures
Install restraints with appropriate release mechanisms in all passenger seats
Evaluate shoulder harness retrofit installations to ensure they reduce the risk of occupant injury
key_findings: Intentional understatement of helicopter empty weight by Carson Helicopters, Inc.
Alteration of power available chart to exaggerate lift capability
Use of unapproved torque in performance calculations
Inadequate training and oversight leading to reliance on inaccurate performance data
Immediate post-crash fire due to non-crash resistant fuel tanks
Separation of cabin seats from floor during crash
Improper release mechanism on seat restraints

flight_type: 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 135 on-demand charter operation
probable_cause: the captain's decision to attempt a go-around late in the landing roll with insufficient runway remaining.
contributing_factors: pilots' poor crew coordination and lack of cockpit discipline
fatigue, which likely impaired both pilots' performance
failure of the Federal Aviation Administration to require crew resource management training and standard operating procedures for Part 135 operators

cockpit discipline
pilot fatigue
standard operating procedures
checklist execution
sterile cockpit adherence
wet runway landing distances
line checks adequacy
terrain database updates
recommendations: Require manufacturers of newly certificated and in-service turbine-powered aircraft to incorporate a committed-to-stop point in the Aircraft Flight Manuals.
Require operators and training schools to incorporate information from revised Aircraft Flight Manuals into their manuals and training.
Require Part 135 and 91 subpart K operators to establish standard operating procedures.
Ensure pilots use same checklists in operations as used during training for normal, abnormal, and emergency conditions.
Design or revise checklists to require clear call out of actual flap position.
Revise Advisory Circular 00-24B with explanations of terms describing severe thunderstorms.
Permit appropriate use of prescription sleep medications by pilots under medical supervision for insomnia.
Require Part 135 and 91 subpart K pilots to receive initial and recurrent education on fatigue factors, signs, symptoms, and management strategies.
Review policy standards for common sleep-related conditions in accordance with current scientific evidence.
Increase education and training of physicians and pilots on common sleep disorders.
Pursue development of technology to reduce or prevent runway excursions and require its installation once available.
Inform operators about potential underestimation of landing distance required on wet, ungrooved runways and provide guidance for conducting landing distance assessments.
Require that Part 135 pilot-in-command line checks be conducted independently from other required checks and represent typical revenue operations.
Ensure terrain avoidance warning system-equipped aircraft have the current terrain database installed.
key_findings: Captain's decision to attempt a go-around late in landing roll with insufficient runway remaining
Pilots' poor crew coordination and lack of cockpit discipline
Fatigue likely impaired both pilots' performance
FAA did not require crew resource management training and standard operating procedures for Part 135 operators

operator: FedEx Corporation and operated by Empire Airlines, Inc
probable_cause: The flight crew's failure to monitor and maintain a minimum safe airspeed while executing an instrument approach in icing conditions, which resulted in an aerodynamic stall at low altitude.
contributing_factors: Flight crew's failure to follow published standard operating procedures in response to a flap anomaly
Captain's decision to continue with the unstabilized approach
Poor crew resource management
Fatigue due to the time of day and cumulative sleep debt, which likely impaired the captain's performance
safety_issues: Lack of low-airspeed alerting system on the airplane
Steep authority gradient in the cockpit and minimal training on assertiveness for first officers
Need for best practices for conducting single and multiple emergency situations at low altitudes
Dispatching and operating an airplane in known icing conditions without certification or demonstrated ability to operate safely
recommendations: Require role-playing or simulator-based exercises that teach first officers assertiveness and captains leadership styles supporting first officer assertiveness as part of crew resource management training for Part 121, 135, and 91 subpart K pilots
Prohibit dispatching or deliberate operation in known freezing rain or drizzle unless the airplane manufacturer has demonstrated safe operations in those conditions

Review approved pilot, dispatcher, and flight follower training programs to include standardized training on dangers of flight operations in freezing precipitation and differences between ground deicing considerations and in-flight icing operations

Develop a method for quickly communicating information regarding persons on board and presence of hazardous materials to emergency responders when airport emergency response or search and rescue is activated
Amend Advisory Circular 150/5200-30C to include guidance on monitoring and ensuring operability of emergency response and mutual aid gates during winter operations
Require all operators of ATR 42- and ATR 72-series airplanes to retrofit with an aircraft performance monitoring system if not already equipped
Require all ATR 42-series airplanes to be equipped with a flap asymmetry annunciator light if not already so equipped
Define and codify minimum simulator model fidelity requirements for aerodynamic degradations resulting from airframe ice accumulation

Once the simulator model fidelity requirements are implemented, require flight crews of all aircraft certificated for flight in icing conditions to be trained in simulators meeting these fidelity requirements
key_findings: The first officer's failure to maintain airspeed while acting as pilot flying likely resulted from distraction by flap anomaly and captain's actions
Captain's failure to call out the first officer's airspeed deviations due to preoccupation with inappropriate, nonstandard procedure in response to flap anomaly
Sufficient reference for maintaining minimum safe airspeed despite some airspeed bugs not set to appropriate approach speeds
Reliance on flight crew vigilance and stall warning systems may be inadequate to prevent hazardous low-airspeed situations

flight_type: 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 91 flight operations
probable_cause: the pilot's temporary unresponsiveness for reasons that could not be established from the available information.
contributing_factors: lack of a cockpit recorder system with the ability to capture audio, images, and parametric data.
safety_issues: Automated weather sensor system site deficiencies
Lack of cockpit recorder systems in turbine-powered aircraft
Emergency locator transmitter retention capabilities
recommendations: Require a detailed inspection during annual inspections to ensure emergency locator transmitters are mounted and retained according to manufacturer's specifications.
Revise internal FAA guidance on medical certification following stroke or intracerebral hemorrhage to include specific requirements for neuropsychological evaluation.
Correct deficiencies with in-service automated weather sensor system stations, specifically present weather sensors and ceilometers.

Implement a collaborative test program between the FAA, NWS, local academic community, and private entities to establish viability of relaying weather information collected from airborne aircraft equipped with existing data-link technology.
Encourage and provide incentives for data link-equipped aircraft operators in Alaska to outfit their aircraft with weather-sensing equipment for real-time data relay.
Educate pilots about the benefits of notifying passengers about the location and operation of survival and emergency communication equipment on board airplanes.
key_findings: The airplane was in a climbing left turn when it collided with terrain, and flight control inputs occurred shortly before terrain impact.

A crash-resistant flight recorder system that captures cockpit audio, images, and parametric data would have substantially aided investigators in determining the circumstances that led to this accident.
Had the emergency locator transmitter remained attached to its mounting tray, rescue personnel could have reached the survivors hours earlier.

location: Near Santa Fe, New Mexico June
probable_cause: The pilot's decision to take off from a remote, mountainous landing site in dark (moonless) night, windy, instrument meteorological conditions.
contributing_factors: An organizational culture that prioritized mission execution over aviation safety
Pilot fatigue
Self-induced pressure to conduct the flight
Situational stress
safety_issues: Deficiencies in New Mexico State Police (NMSP) aviation section's safety-related policies, including lack of a requirement for risk assessment during missions
Inadequate pilot staffing and fatigue management program
Lack of effective communication procedures between airborne and ground personnel during SAR missions
recommendations: Require the New Mexico Department of Public Safety to bring its aviation section policies and operations into conformance with industry standards, such as those established by the Airborne Law Enforcement Association.
Develop and implement a comprehensive fatigue management program for NMSP aviation section pilots that requires protected rest periods
Revise or reinforce SAR policies to ensure direct communication between NMSP aviation units and ground teams during missions

Airborne Law Enforcement Association should revise standards to define pilot rest and ensure adequate rest periods, require training in exiting instrument meteorological conditions, encourage installation of 406-MHz ELTs and flight-tracking equipment on all aircraft.

National Association of State Aviation Officials and International Association of Chiefs of Police should encourage members to review policies and procedures for alignment with safety standards and guidelines.
key_findings: The helicopter was properly certificated and maintained, with no evidence of preimpact structural or system failures
No evidence that the pilot had any medical or toxicological condition affecting performance during the flight
Pilot and hiker were ejected from the helicopter due to forces beyond seat and restraint systems' certification limits during the roll down the mountainside
Ground SAR teams could not reach the pilot before he died of exposure given adverse weather conditions, darkness, rugged terrain, distance to travel, and severity of injuries

probable_cause: (1) the pilot's failure to ensure that a fuel system icing inhibitor was added to the fuel before the flights on the day of the accident; (2) his failure to take appropriate remedial actions after a low fuel pressure state (resulting from icing within the fuel system) and a lateral fuel imbalance developed, including diverting to a suitable airport before the fuel imbalance became extreme; and (3) a loss of control while the pilot was maneuvering the left-wing-heavy airplane near the approach end of the runway.
contributing_factors: pilot's failure to ensure that a fuel system icing inhibitor was added to the fuel
failure to take appropriate remedial actions after a low fuel pressure state developed
loss of control while maneuvering the left-wing-heavy airplane
safety_issues: fuel system ice accumulation hazard
pilot and operator guidance on use of fuel system icing inhibitors
passenger restraint compliance in Part 91 operations
need for cockpit audio, images, and parametric data recording systems
recommendations: Amend certification requirements for aircraft requiring fuel additives to highlight those limitations by a warning in the limitations section of the airplane flight manual.
Require existing certificated aircraft that require fuel additives to have those limitations highlighted by a warning in the limitations section of the airplane flight manual.

Amend aircraft certification fuel placarding requirements so that aircraft requiring fuel additives have a fuel filler placard noting this limitation and referring to the airplane flight manual for specific information about the limitation.
Issue guidance on fuel system icing prevention including pilot precautions and procedures to avoid fuel system icing problems aboard turbine engine-powered aircraft.
key_findings: The pilot was properly certificated and qualified in accordance with applicable Federal regulations.
The airplane was properly certified, equipped, and maintained in accordance with Federal regulations.
Low fuel pressure state and restricted fuel supply from the left tank during the accident flight were due to ice accumulation in the fuel system.
If a fuel system icing inhibitor had been added as required, the ice accumulation would have been avoided.
The pilot underestimated the seriousness of the initial fuel imbalance indications.

probable_cause: a manufacturing defect in a clutch mechanism that prevented the speedbrakes from automatically deploying after touchdown and the captain's failure to monitor and extend the speedbrakes manually.
contributing_factors: the captain's failure to confirm speedbrake extension before announcing their deployment
his distraction caused by the thrust reversers' failure to initially deploy after landing
safety_issues: Inadequate pilot training for recognition of a situation in which the speedbrakes do not automatically deploy as expected after landing.
Lack of an alert to warn pilots when speedbrakes have not automatically deployed during the landing roll.
Lack of guidance for pilots of certain Boeing airplanes to follow when an unintended thrust reverser lockout occurs.
recommendations: Require all operators of existing speedbrake-equipped transport-category airplanes to develop and incorporate training to specifically address recognition of a situation in which the speedbrakes do not deploy as expected after landing.

Require all newly type-certificated 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 25 airplanes to have a clearly distinguishable and intelligible alert that warns pilots when the speedbrakes have not deployed during the landing roll.
Require Boeing to establish guidance for pilots of all relevant airplanes to follow when an unintended thrust reverser lockout occurs and to provide that guidance to all operators of those airplanes.
key_findings: The incident pilots were certificated in accordance with federal regulations and were current and qualified in the incident airplane.
Both pilots had completed American Airlines' Jackson Hole Airport special airport training and had recent experience flying into JAC.
There was no evidence of fatigue or any medical or behavioral conditions that might have adversely affected the pilots' performance during the incident flight.

probable_cause: An aerodynamic stall and subsequent uncommanded roll during a one-engine-inoperative takeoff flight test, which were the result of (1) Gulfstream's failure to properly develop and validate takeoff speeds for the flight tests and recognize and correct the takeoff safety speed (V2) error during previous G650 flight tests, (2) the G650 flight test team's persistent and increasingly aggressive attempts to achieve V2 speeds that were erroneously low, and (3) Gulfstream's inadequate investigation of previous G650 uncommanded roll events, which indicated that the company's estimated stall angle of attack while the airplane was in ground effect was too high.
contributing_factors: Gulfstream's failure to effectively manage the G650 flight test program by pursuing an aggressive program schedule without ensuring that the roles and responsibilities of team members had been appropriately defined and implemented, engineering processes had received sufficient technical planning and oversight, potential hazards had been fully identified, and appropriate risk controls had been implemented and were functioning as intended.
safety_issues: Deficiencies in Gulfstream's technical planning and oversight contributed to the incorrect speeds used on the day of the accident.
Gulfstream's flight test safety program at the time of the accident was deficient because risk controls were insufficient and safety assurance activities were lacking.
recommendations: Effective flight test standard operating policies and procedures that are fully implemented by manufacturers would help reduce the inherent risks associated with flight testing.
Flight test safety management system guidance specifically tailored to the needs of manufacturers would help promote the development of effective flight test programs.
External safety audits would help Gulfstream monitor the implementation of safety management principles and practices into its flight test operations and sustain long-term cultural change.
key_findings: The airplane stalled at an angle of attack (AOA) that was below the in-ground-effect stall AOA predicted by Gulfstream and the AOA threshold for the activation of the stick shaker stall warning.
Gulfstream's decision to use a takeoff speed development method from a previous airplane program was inappropriate and resulted in target takeoff safety speed values that were too low to be achieved.

probable_cause: Sundance Helicopters' inadequate maintenance of the helicopter, including (1) the improper reuse of a degraded self-locking nut, (2) the improper or lack of installation of a split pin, and (3) inadequate postmaintenance inspections, which resulted in the in-flight separation of the servo control input rod from the fore/aft servo and rendered the helicopter uncontrollable.
contributing_factors: The mechanic's fatigue
Lack of clearly delineated maintenance task steps to follow
Inspector's fatigue
Lack of clearly delineated inspection steps to follow
safety_issues: Improper reuse of degraded self-locking nuts
Inadequate postmaintenance inspections
Fatigue among maintenance personnel
Lack of human factors training for maintenance personnel
Absence of duty-time regulations for maintenance personnel
recommendations: Establish duty-time regulations for maintenance personnel working under 14 Code of Federal Regulations Parts 121, 135, 145, and 91 Subpart K

Encourage operators and manufacturers to develop and implement best practices for conducting maintenance including the use of work cards for maintenance tasks especially those involving safety-critical functions that promote the recording and verification of delineated steps in the task

Require personnel performing maintenance or inspections under 14 Code of Federal Regulations Parts 121, 135, 145, and 91 Subpart K to receive initial and recurrent training on human factors affecting maintenance
key_findings: The fore/aft servo bolt most likely disengaged because the split pin was installed improperly or it was not installed and a self-locking nut that either was degraded or not torqued was used when the fore/aft servo was replaced

Sundance Helicopters did not meet the Tour Operators Program of Safety (TOPS) audit requirements due to misinterpretation of maintenance standards regarding training and qualifications of mechanics by Sundance's director of maintenance
Both the mechanic and inspector were experiencing fatigue during the December 6 shift, which contributed to improper securing of hardware and incomplete inspections

flight_type: 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 135 medical flight
probable_cause: the pilot's failure to confirm that the helicopter had adequate fuel on board to complete the mission before making the first departure, his improper decision to continue the mission and make a second departure after he became aware of a critically low fuel level, and his failure to successfully enter an autorotation when the engine lost power due to fuel exhaustion
contributing_factors: the pilot's distracted attention due to personal texting during safety-critical ground and flight operations
his degraded performance due to fatigue
the operator's lack of a policy requiring that an operational control center specialist be notified of abnormal fuel situations
the lack of practice representative of an actual engine failure at cruise airspeed in the pilot's autorotation training in the accident make and model helicopter
safety_issues: Distraction due to nonoperational use of portable electronic devices during flight and ground operations.
Lack of Air Methods Operational Control Center involvement in decision-making
Inadequate guidance on autorotation entry procedures
Need for simulator training of helicopter emergency medical services pilots
Lack of a flight recorder
recommendations: Require emergency medical services operators to use formalized dispatch and flight-following procedures that include up-to-date weather information and assistance in flight risk assessment decisions.

Develop criteria for scenario-based helicopter emergency medical services (HEMS) pilot training that includes inadvertent flight into instrument meteorological conditions and hazards unique to HEMS operations, and determine how frequently this training is required to ensure proficiency.

Once the actions recommended in Safety Recommendation A-09-87 are completed, require helicopter emergency medical services pilots to undergo periodic FAA-approved scenario-based simulator training, including training that makes use of simulators or flight training devices.
key_findings: The pilot and the helicopter were properly certified for the 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 135 helicopter emergency medical services flight.
Wreckage examination determined that the helicopter's engine lost power due to fuel exhaustion.
Although the helicopter's low fuel state was clearly indicated, the pilot missed three opportunities to detect the condition.

flight_type: Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) with a visual approach clearance
probable_cause: The flight crew's mismanagement of the airplane's vertical profile during the initial approach, which led to increased workload and reduced awareness of the AFDS and A/T modes. This resulted in an inadvertent deactivation of automatic airspeed control and failure to initiate a timely go-around when low path and airspeed conditions were recognized.
contributing_factors: Flight crew fatigue
Nonstandard communication and coordination between pilot flying (PF) and pilot monitoring (PM)
Role confusion during the approach phase
Complexities in the Boeing 777 automatic flight control system (AFCS) and inadequacies in related training and documentation
safety_issues: Automation reliance leading to insufficient manual monitoring of airspeed indications
Delayed initiation of a go-around after recognizing low path and airspeed conditions
Role confusion between PF and PM during the approach phase
Inadequate understanding of AFCS interactions by flight crew
recommendations: Review and improve training programs for Boeing 777 automatic flight control systems, focusing on interaction between autopilot flight director system (AFDS) and autothrottle (A/T)
Develop clearer documentation to help pilots understand the complexities of AFCS
Implement measures to reduce pilot fatigue during long-haul flights
Enhance communication protocols for nonstandard situations involving AFDS and A/T mode selections
key_findings: The flight crew's mismanagement of vertical profile led to increased workload and reduced PM awareness around the time of unintended deactivation of automatic airspeed control.
Fatigue likely degraded performance during approach phase.
Nonstandard communication and coordination between PF and PM contributed to role confusion and decreased situational awareness.
Insufficient monitoring of airspeed indications due to expectancy, increased workload, fatigue, and automation reliance.

flight_type: 14 Code of Federal Regulations Part 121 flight
probable_cause: The flight crew's continuation of an unstabilized approach and their failure to monitor the aircraft's altitude during the approach, which led to an inadvertent descent below the minimum approach altitude and subsequently into terrain.
contributing_factors: the flight crew's failure to properly configure and verify the flight management computer for the profile approach
the captain's failure to communicate his intentions to the first officer once it became apparent the vertical profile was not captured
the flight crew's expectation that they would break out of the clouds at 1,000 feet above ground level due to incomplete weather information
the first officer's failure to make the required minimums callouts
the captain's performance deficiencies likely due to factors including, but not limited to, fatigue, distraction, or confusion, consistent with performance deficiencies exhibited during training
the first officer's fatigue due to acute sleep loss resulting from her ineffective off-duty time management and circadian factors
safety_issues: Communication between pilots and dispatchers
Pilot monitoring duties and altitude callouts
Fatigue management among flight crew members
Weather information dissemination, including pertinent remarks about variable cloud ceilings
Approach procedures and training for nonprecision approaches
recommendations: Require all 14 CFR Part 121 and 135 operators to incorporate the constant-angle-of-descent technique into nonprecision approach procedures and to emphasize the preference for that technique where practicable.
key_findings: The flight crew did not monitor the descent rate and continued to fly the airplane with a vertical descent rate of 1,500 ft per minute below 1,000 ft above ground level
The captain's change to a vertical speed approach after failing to capture the profile glidepath was not in accordance with UPS procedures and guidance

The flight crew did not sufficiently monitor the airplane's altitude during the approach and subsequently allowed the airplane to descend below the minimum descent altitude without having the runway environment in sight

flight_type: search and rescue (SAR) flight under 14 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Part 91
probable_cause: the pilot's decision to continue flight under visual flight rules into deteriorating weather conditions, which resulted in the pilot's spatial disorientation and loss of control. Also causal was the Alaska Department of Public Safety's punitive culture and inadequate safety management, which prevented the organization from identifying and correcting latent deficiencies in risk management and pilot training.
contributing_factors: the pilot's exceptionally high motivation to complete search and rescue missions, which increased his risk tolerance and adversely affected his decision-making
safety_issues: lack of organizational policies and procedures for managing operational risks
reliance on non-aviation-trained dispatchers for flight-following support
inadequate training for recognizing hazards posed by precipitation and low light conditions to night vision goggles operations
need for initial and recurrent training on how to recognize, avoid, and safely recover from inadvertent instrument meteorological conditions (IMC)
lack of a formal tactical flight officer program
punitive culture that impeded the free flow of safety-related information
recommendations: require image recording devices on board certain aircraft

implement an effective flight risk evaluation program, formalized dispatch and flight-following procedures, night vision goggles and inadvertent IMC training for pilots, a formal tactical flight officer program, and a comprehensive safety management system
update Federal Aviation Administration publications to include accurate information about attitude indication limitations
key_findings: the helicopter was operating normally throughout the flight with no mechanical abnormalities identified
soon after departure from the remote landing site, the helicopter likely encountered instrument meteorological conditions (IMC) including low clouds, heavy snow, and near-zero-visibility conditions
the pilot experienced a total loss of external visual references while operating in close proximity to terrain, which led him to attempt to transition to instrument flight
the pilot's action to cage the attitude indicator outside those conditions under which it could be safely caged indicates that he distrusted the information he was seeing

probable_cause: The flight crew's failure to maintain adequate airspeed after shutting down the right engine due to an in-flight fire in one of the right augmentors. The failure to maintain airspeed resulted in either an aerodynamic stall or a loss of directional control.
contributing_factors: Exceeding maximum allowable takeoff weight due to non-use of ADI and autofeather systems
Inadequate Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) oversight of Fresh Air's recordkeeping and operations
Captain suffering from acute sleep loss, negatively affecting his ability to guide the first officer in handling the emergency
safety_issues: Deficient recordkeeping by Fresh Air and inadequate FAA oversight
Multiple FAA inspectors failed to perform effective oversight of Fresh Air due to a belief that Part 125 operations merit less scrutiny than Parts 121 and 135 operations
FAA's failure to ensure pilots were evaluated on their knowledge of the certificate holder's OpsSpecs
recommendations: Evaluate the effectiveness of your 14 CFR Part 125 oversight program and ensure that 14 CFR Part 125 operations are conducted at the same level of safety as Parts 121 and 135
Require all principal operations inspectors of 14 CFR Part 125 certificate holders to conduct at least one en route inspection annually on each airplane type operated by the certificate holder

Require check airmen who evaluate pilots under the 14 CFR Part 125 lateral moves provision to use the operations specifications of the certificate holder employing the pilot to ensure proper evaluation of the pilot's knowledge
key_findings: The flight crew likely did not use ADI and autofeather systems during takeoff, resulting in exceeding maximum allowable takeoff weight
Thermal damage resulted from ignition of a flammable liquid in one of the right augmentors due to a leak near the augmentor/muffler junction
FAA's oversight was inadequate regarding Fresh Air's recordkeeping and operations

probable_cause: National Airlines' inadequate procedures for restraining special cargo loads, which resulted in the loadmaster's improper restraint of the cargo, which moved aft and damaged hydraulic systems Nos. 1 and 2 and horizontal stabilizer drive mechanism components, rendering the airplane uncontrollable.
contributing_factors: The Federal Aviation Administration's inadequate oversight of National Airlines' handling of special cargo loads
safety_issues: Deficient procedures for securing special cargo loads
Lack of proper training for loadmaster in securing heavy vehicle special cargo
Insufficient FAA oversight and guidance on cargo handling personnel
recommendations: Certification of personnel responsible for ensuring the proper loading, restraint, and documentation of special cargo loads, including requirements for their procedures, training, and duty time and hour limitations.
Clear guidance regarding Federal Aviation Administration inspector responsibility for the oversight of cargo handling personnel
Alternative method of risk reduction when circumstances such as FAA inspector travel restrictions or resource shortfalls result in the repeated deferral of required surveillance tasks
key_findings: Improper restraint methods allowed the rear mine-resistant ambush-protected all-terrain vehicle to move aft and damage critical components
Cargo movement during previous flight was not recognized as a serious problem by crewmembers and loadmaster
National Airlines' procedures for securing special cargo loads were deficient, even if followed correctly

location:
Near Koehn Dry Lake, California

October
probable_cause: Scaled Composites' failure to consider and protect against the possibility that a single human error could result in a catastrophic hazard to the SpaceShipTwo vehicle, leading to the copilot's premature unlocking of the feather system due to time pressure and unfamiliarity with vibration and loads.
contributing_factors: high workload on the copilot
lack of rigorous verification and validation of mitigation measures against human error
safety_issues: inadequate systems safety analysis by Scaled Composites
Federal Aviation Administration's (FAA) Office of Commercial Space Transportation's deficient evaluations of hazard analyses in experimental permit applications
need for improved emergency readiness procedures
recommendations: improving the design, operational procedures, and flight crew simulator training to reduce human error risks
enhancing FAA's inspection process by assigning inspectors to commercial space operators rather than individual operations
creating a database of lessons learned from commercial space mishap investigations

FAA's evaluations failed to recognize that Scaled Composites' hazard analysis did not meet regulatory requirements for identifying hazards caused by human error
human factors should be emphasized in commercial space vehicle design and operations

probable_cause: The flight crewmembers' failure to perform the flight control check before takeoff, their attempt to take off with the gust lock system engaged, and their delayed execution of a rejected takeoff after they became aware that the controls were locked.
contributing_factors: the flight crew's habitual noncompliance with checklists
Gulfstream Aerospace Corporation's failure to ensure that the G-IV gust lock/throttle lever interlock system would prevent an attempted takeoff with the gust lock engaged
the Federal Aviation Administration's failure to detect this inadequacy during the G-IV's certification
safety_issues: Habitual noncompliance with standard operating procedures by flight crew

Cessna Citation airplanes did not have a cockpit annunciation to alert pilots that the parking brake was set or an unambiguous checklist item to direct pilots to fully release the parking brake before takeoff.

safety_issues: inflight separation of the tail boom due to improperly installed or fractured attachment hardware

flight_type: Title 14 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Part 135 scheduled passenger flight
probable_cause: the in-flight unthreading of the clamp nut from the horizontal stabilizer trim actuator barrel due to a missing lock ring, which resulted in the horizontal stabilizer moving to an extreme trailing-edge-down position rendering the airplane's pitch uncontrollable.
contributing_factors: missing or damaged lock ring
installation of a moisture seal deviating from manufacturer's maintenance procedures
safety_issues: need for secondary retention feature on horizontal stabilizer trim actuator
potential hazard of installing an unapproved moisture seal
lack of clear and concise guidance regarding inspection and maintenance procedures
recommendations: ensure presence of lock ring in the horizontal stabilizer trim actuator assembly
install a secondary retention feature on the horizontal stabilizer trim actuator
provide clear and concise guidance from the manufacturer regarding the horizontal stabilizer actuator inspection and maintenance procedures
key_findings: failure in the horizontal stabilizer trim actuator caused the airplane to abruptly pitch down during the accident sequence, from which recovery was not possible.
deformation of the inner diameter of the lock ring or condition of the tang can prevent it from retaining the clamp nut as intended by design.

flight_type: Title 14 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Part 121 scheduled international passenger flight
probable_cause: the American Airlines flight 106 (AAL106) crew's surface navigation error due to distractions caused by their performance of concurrent operational tasks during taxi, which resulted in a loss of situational awareness.
contributing_factors: the air traffic control tower team's nondetection of the AAL106 crew 's deviation from taxi instructions while performing concurrent operational tasks
the timing of the runway status light system, which activated too late to prevent the AAL106 crew from crossing the runway hold short line
American Airlines' lack of adequate risk controls to prevent concurrent flight crew tasks from leading to distraction, loss of situational awareness, and deviation from an authorized taxi clearance
safety_issues: the need for additional risk mitigation strategies to prevent flight crew surface navigation errors that result in runway incursions
the need for a procedural crosscheck that requires flight crews to verbalize the number of a runway they are about to cross as indicated by runway signs
the lack of flight deck technology to detect potential traffic conflicts
recommendations: implementing additional risk mitigation strategies as part of an operator's safety management system to reduce the likelihood that flight crew performance of concurrent tasks during taxi will lead to inaccurate navigation on the airport by reducing distractions associated with multitasking

the implementation of a flight deck alerting system on transport-category aircraft that provides alerts of traffic on a runway or taxiway and traffic on approach to land would enhance safety by providing pilots with improved situational awareness and would reduce the risk of future runway-related incidents and accidents
key_findings: the captain's deviation from American Airlines flight 106's taxi clearance likely resulted from several factors, including an early clearance to cross runway 31L, interruptions and multitasking related to the crew's delayed receipt of the load closeout, the captain's prospective memory error in forgetting to turn right at taxiway K, and environmental capture

the first officer and relief first officer were likely distracted from their primary duty of assisting the captain in safely taxiing the airplane by other operational activities, which resulted in the crew's loss of situational awareness during a critical phase of flight

probable_cause: the local controller's incorrect assumption that the Southwest Airlines (SWA) airplane would depart from the runway before the Federal Express airplane arrived on the same runway, which resulted in a loss of separation between both airplanes.
contributing_factors: his expectation bias regarding the SWA airplane's departure
his lack of situational awareness regarding the SWA airplane's position when the flight crew requested takeoff clearance
the air traffic control tower's lack of training (before the incident) on low-visibility operations
SWA flight crewmembers' failure to account for the traffic that was on short final approach and to notify the controller that they would need additional time on the runway before the takeoff roll
FAA's failure to require surface detection equipment at Austin-Bergstrom International Airport and direct alerting for flight crews
safety_issues: lack of surface detection equipment at AUS to alert controllers about potential conflicts on a taxiway or runway surface
need for flight deck technology to alert flight crews about potential conflicts on an airport surface
need to ensure, especially during low-visibility conditions, that controllers are aware when pilots might need extra time on the runway after receiving takeoff clearance
lack of training on AUS airport's Surface Movement Guidance and Control System plan
need for low-visibility operations training at all airports
recommendations: require surface detection equipment at Austin-Bergstrom International Airport
implement a flight deck alerting system on air carrier aircraft
provide refresher training on low-visibility operations to air traffic control tower personnel
install cockpit voice recorders (CVR) with a 25-hour recording capability
key_findings: controller's inaccurate mental model of the SWA airplane's position on the taxiway resulted from his expectation bias and failure to verify the SWA airplane's position
SWA flight crewmembers should have informed the controller about their plan to perform an engine run-up
controllers need to consider ambient conditions when providing takeoff clearance

flight_type: Title 14 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Part 91
probable_cause: the operator's failure to adequately inspect the former military turbine-powered helicopter, which allowed an engine issue to progress and result in a loss of engine power and a subsequent loss of control after the helicopter struck powerlines during a forced landing.
contributing_factors: the FAA's inadequate inspection and maintenance standards for former military turbine-powered aircraft operating with an experimental exhibition airworthiness certificate
the operator's use of those standards instead of more rigorous standards, which were readily available to the operator and previously used to inspect and maintain the helicopter
the FAA's inadequate oversight of the operator, which did not detect the inherent risk associated with the operation
safety_issues: Federal Aviation Administration's (FAA) inspection requirements for the UH-1B and other former military turbine-powered aircraft
operator's maintenance of the accident helicopter
operator's management of the helicopter's experimental airworthiness certificate
FAA's oversight of the certificate
recommendations: As a result of this investigation, the National Transportation Safety Board makes the following new safety recommendations.
The National Transportation Safety Board reiterates the following safety recommendation.
key_findings: The helicopter engine lost power due to degraded radial support for the power turbine shaft at the No. 3 and No. 4 bearings due to cracks in the exhaust diffuser and a separation of the rear bearing cover outer flange.
FAA's inspection standards did not have sufficient scope and detail to ensure airworthiness of former military turbine-powered rotorcraft.
Operator should have used more stringent inspection procedures that were previously used.

flight_type: Title 14 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Part 91 business flight
probable_cause: the flight crew's decision to take off in tailwind conditions that were consistently above the airplane's tailwind limitation, which resulted in a runway overrun following an aborted takeoff.
contributing_factors: flight crew's use of the instantaneous wind report for the decision to attempt the takeoff
safety_issues: lack of official definition and guidance on the use of instantaneous wind reports during flight operations
recommendations: As a result of this investigation, the National Transportation Safety Board makes the following new safety recommendation.
key_findings: The probable cause was the flight crew's decision to take off in tailwind conditions that were consistently above the airplane's tailwind limitation.
Contributing was the flight crew's use of the instantaneous wind report for the decision to attempt the takeoff.

operator: eastern standard time, the flight crew of United Airlines
contributing_factors: restricted rudder control system in airplanes equipped with Collins Aerospace SVO-730 rudder rollout guidance actuators with incorrectly assembled bearings
safety_issues: potential for a jammed or restricted rudder control system on certain Boeing 737 airplanes
excessive rudder input if the jam is cleared by responding with Boeing's mitigation
recommendations: Informing Boeing 737 flight crews of the potential for a restricted rudder control system and appropriate mitigations
Removing Collins Aerospace SVO-730 rudder rollout guidance actuators with incorrectly assembled bearings from Boeing 737 airplanes
key_findings: Rudder pedals were stuck in their neutral position during landing rollout, not moving in response to foot pressure.
Application of up to 75 pounds of force on the rudder pedals did not clear the jam until airspeed was less than 20 knots.

contributing_factors: Insufficient separation distances between helicopter traffic on Route 4 and aircraft landing on runway 33
safety_issues: Increased risk of midair collision due to insufficient separation distances
recommendations: Prohibition of Route 4 operations as recommended in Safety Recommendation A-25-1
Development of an alternate route for public safety helicopter operations without increasing controller workload
key_findings: Existing separation distances between helicopter traffic operating on Route 4 and aircraft landing on runway 33 are insufficient and pose an intolerable risk to aviation safety.

contributing_factors: failure of the left hydraulic system
unairworthy bannis latches that don't comply with Airworthiness Directives
safety_issues: delayed evacuation during an emergency due to jammed slides
incorrectly routed deployment strap preventing door from opening
recommendations: Boeing and FAA take action on the safety recommendations in this report addressing the failure of an evacuation slide to deploy normally during an emergency evacuation.

location: Flight Separation of Left Mid Exit Door Plug, Alaska
probable_cause: The in-flight separation of the left mid exit door (MED) plug due to Boeing Commercial Airplanes' failure to provide adequate training, guidance, and oversight necessary to ensure that manufacturing personnel could consistently and correctly comply with its parts removal process.
contributing_factors: The Federal Aviation Administration's ineffective compliance enforcement surveillance and audit planning activities, which failed to adequately identify and ensure that Boeing addressed the repetitive and systemic nonconformance issues associated with its parts removal process.
safety_issues: Boeing Commercial Airplanes' Business Process Instruction 'Perform Part or Assembly Removal' lacked clarity, conciseness, and ease of use necessary for manufacturing personnel.
On-the-job training at Boeing was unstructured, undocumented, and focused primarily on routine build tasks.
The absence of a removal record indicating that the left mid exit door plug installation had been disturbed led to no quality assurance inspection being performed.
Hands-on oxygen mask simulator training provided to flight crews lacked realistic scenario-based exercises.
Alaska Airlines' procedures at the time of the accident were ineffective in ensuring cockpit voice recorder data preservation from the accident flight.
recommendations: Implement a fully developed safety management system, integrated into Boeing Commercial Airplanes' quality management and production processes to proactively identify and manage human risks.
Ensure that hands-on, aircraft-specific crew training includes realistic scenario-based exercises for using each type of oxygen system in an operator's fleet.
Review and potentially revise Federal Aviation Administration design standards or mask designs to ensure ease of use and quick donning in emergencies.
Implement cockpit voice recorders (CVR) with a 25-hour recording capability.
Mandate the usage of child restraint systems for children under the age of 2 years on board aircraft.
key_findings: The left mid exit door plug's vertical movement arrestor bolts and upper guide track bolts were missing before the plug moved upward off the stop pads.
Flight attendant A faced difficulties in communicating with the flight crew but this did not impede cabin procedures post-rapid depressurization.
All flight attendants and passengers used overhead oxygen masks effectively after rapid depressurization, and portable oxygen bottles provided necessary support.

flight_type: Title 14 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) Part 135 cargo flight
probable_cause: The pilot's inability to maintain control of the airplane when it encountered a microburst during a landing attempt and a go-around near known thunderstorm activity.
contributing_factors: Inadequate amount of wind sensors and wind shear detection equipment used by air traffic control (ATC) tower to detect microburst activity at the airport.
safety_issues: Enhancement of current wind detection capabilities to improve tower controllers' awareness of wind-related hazards and their ability to alert pilots before departure and arrival.
recommendations: Improve wind detection capabilities at Salt Lake City International Airport
key_findings: The airport's automated surface observing system (ASOS) reported higher winds than the ATC tower, but this information was not accessible to ATC or the pilot.

contributing_factors: pitting corrosion along the retainer ring groove corners on the interior of the actuator housing
safety_issues: failure to fully extend main landing gear
loss of hydraulic fluid due to housing fracture
recommendations: inspection and overhaul of the hydraulic actuator at a regular interval
key_findings: fatigue cracks initiated from pitting corrosion along the retainer ring groove corners on the interior of the actuator housing
